systems.name: Temporal
systems.authoring_model: Code-first in a general-purpose language: Go, Java, TypeScript, Python, .NET, PHP, Ruby. Core abstraction = a plain function/method that IS the workflow. A workflow is deterministic orchestration code the engine replays from durable Event History; side effects live in separate Activity functions. Minimal Go workflow: `func MoneyTransfer(ctx workflow.Context, input PaymentDetails) (string, error)` — first param is the special `workflow.Context`, input is a single serializable struct (docs strongly recommend ONE struct over positional params so you can add fields later without breaking history). Inside you must set `workflow.ActivityOptions{StartToCloseTimeout, RetryPolicy}`, wrap the context with `workflow.WithActivityOptions(ctx, opts)`, then `workflow.ExecuteActivity(ctx, Withdraw, input).Get(ctx, &out)` — the `.Get(ctx, &out)` blocks-and-decodes idiom is everywhere. Activities are ordinary functions taking stdlib `context.Context` (NOT workflow.Context). Nothing runs until you register on a Worker bound to a task queue: `w := worker.New(c, taskQueue, opts); w.RegisterWorkflow(MoneyTransfer); w.RegisterActivity(Withdraw); w.Run(...)`. TypeScript is the most ergonomic: workflows are async functions, activities are proxied via `proxyActivities<typeof activities>({startToCloseTimeout})` giving type-safe `await activities.foo()`. Python uses `@workflow.defn`/`@workflow.run` and `@activity.defn` decorators. Java uses an interface + `@WorkflowMethod`/`@ActivityMethod` annotations plus a separate impl class.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: AUTOMATIC and this is a major strength vs Aion's Gleam codec tax. A DataConverter runs input/output through an ordered chain of PayloadConverters (Nil/Undefined, ByteSlice/Binary, ProtoJSON, Proto, JSON) — first one that handles the value wins. In practice: pass any JSON-serializable value and it just works; source of truth is your native TYPES (Go structs w/ json tags, TS interfaces, Python dataclasses, Java POJOs) — no hand-written encoder/decoder, no separate schema file. Language-specific reality: Go uses encoding/json reflection (unexported fields silently dropped; needs json tags). Python's default converter handles None, bytes, protobuf, dataclasses, UUID, IntEnum/StrEnum and anything json.dumps supports; docs 'strongly encourage a single dataclass or Pydantic model' for forward-compat. TypeScript is the leakiest: default is UndefinedPayloadConverter→BinaryPayloadConverter→JsonPayloadConverter (plain JSON.stringify), so Date, Map, Set, BigInt, RegExp, Uint8Array, and class instances do NOT round-trip — Date silently becomes a string, requiring a custom EJSON/superjson converter. Python similarly needs the opt-in `temporalio.contrib.pydantic.pydantic_data_converter` for datetime.date/time/datetime and full Pydantic types. Custom DataConverters are also the extension point for encryption/compression codecs. Net: ~0 lines of serialization boilerplate per type in the common case — the exact pain Aion's Gleam side has, solved by host-language reflection/serde that Gleam lacks.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability via event sourcing: every workflow decision (activity call, timer, signal, side-effect) is appended to a durable Event History; on worker crash/restart the workflow function is RE-EXECUTED from the start and the SDK feeds recorded results back instead of re-running side effects (replay). The author is heavily burdened by determinism: workflow code must be deterministic, so you may NOT call wall-clock time, random, UUIDs, goroutines/threads, network/IO, or iterate maps in random order directly — you must use the SDK's deterministic replacements (workflow.Now, workflow.SideEffect, workflow.Go, workflow.Sleep, deterministic Sets). Determinism is checked at replay; violations throw NonDeterministicException, and — the classic footgun — bugs often DON'T surface at first run, only weeks later when a long-running workflow replays against changed code ('Temporal replay bugs hide in plain sight'). Versioning is the notorious pain: to change deployed workflow code you either (a) patch with `workflow.GetVersion(ctx, changeID, DefaultVersion, 1)` and branch on the returned version — which accumulates many stale `if version==` branches over time that are 'challenging to manage' and risky to remove — or (b) use the newer Worker Versioning (pin whole workflows to a code version so old executions finish on old workers), plus Replay Testing in CI (WorkflowReplayer replays real histories against new code). Newer `ExecuteWithVersion` API separates writing a change from activating it. Versioning + non-determinism is consistently the #1 source of forum threads and production incidents.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Per-workflow ceremony is real but modest and mostly IS intent (options/timeouts/retries are meaningful). A minimal Go workflow is ~15-25 lines of which ~8 are ActivityOptions/RetryPolicy/WithActivityOptions plumbing + the `.Get(ctx, &out)` unwrap dance per activity. The heavier tax is STRUCTURAL: you always maintain (1) workflow fn, (2) activity fns, (3) a worker main that registers everything on a task queue, (4) a starter/client to kick it off — four moving parts and two processes minimum, plus for tests a suite struct with SetupTest/AfterTest. Crucially, the SERIALIZATION boilerplate that dominates Aion's Gleam workflows is ~ZERO here because the host language gives it free (serde/reflection). So Temporal's ceremony is 'distributed-systems config' (queues, timeouts, retries, registration) rather than 'describe your data three times'. TS reduces the ceremony most (proxyActivities + async/await reads almost like normal code).
systems.visual_editor: NO first-party visual authoring. Explicitly code-first by design; the official Temporal Web UI is an OBSERVABILITY/operations surface only — see/search/replay executions, view Event History, inspect pending activities — 'never meant to be a workflow authoring tool.' Third-party visual layers exist and are positioned as a missing gap: Synergy Codes' open-source 'Workflow Builder' (Apache-2.0 React/React-Flow SDK) draws/serializes a workflow graph that maps onto Temporal, and there are npm temporal-workflow-ui projects — but the graph builder 'does not run one'; the runtime stays code. Community forum threads asking for a workflow-authoring UI are answered with 'that's not what Temporal is.' This is a clear differentiation opening for Aion (code-first runtime + first-party visual authoring/round-trip).
systems.agent_support: Strong and very recent — a deliberate strategic push into 'durable AI agents' (2025). First-party OpenAI Agents SDK integration (Public Preview, announced with OpenAI, Sept 2025): an `OpenAIAgentsPlugin` wraps the agent's reasoning loop inside a Temporal workflow, each LLM call and tool call becomes a durable Activity, and `activity_as_tool` auto-generates OpenAI-compatible tool schemas from an activity's function signature. Also official integrations with Vercel's AI SDK and Pydantic AI (durable_execution). Value prop: automatic survival of LLM rate limits, network blips, and worker crashes with no extra code, plus full replayable history of an agent run. Observability/intervention is via the same general Web UI (view history, send Signals, terminate) — NOT a purpose-built live agent transcript/intervention plane like Aion's NOI. So Aion's differentiator is a native real-time agent transcript + mid-run inject/intervene ops-console, vs Temporal's 'agents are just durable workflows + generic UI'.
top_pain_points: Non-determinism / replay errors are the dominant complaint: changing deployed workflow code (adding an activity, a sleep, or logic inside a signal handler) breaks replay of in-flight executions with NonDeterministicException — and it often surfaces days/weeks later, not at deploy (multiple community.temporal.io threads: 'Non-determinism after Versioning', 'Need help with temporal versioning production issue', 'Trouble with non-determinism').

Versioning ergonomics: GetVersion() branching bloats workflows with stale `if version == DefaultVersion` code paths that are hard to read, hard to know when it's safe to remove (dedicated forum thread), and easy to get wrong (which changeID, what maxSupported value).

Steep learning curve: you must internalize workers, task queues, activities, signals, queries, determinism, and replay semantics before being productive; widely called 'not suitable for teams new to async/concurrent systems.' Reported median time-to-first-prod-workflow ~11 days (Go/Python teams) to ~26 days (teams coming from Airflow).

Self-hosting operational overhead: the server needs a database (Postgres/MySQL/Cassandra) plus typically Elasticsearch for visibility and 4+ internal services, all HA-provisioned for peak — heavy ops burden that pushes many toward paid Temporal Cloud.

Language-specific serialization gotchas despite auto-conversion: TypeScript's default JSON converter silently mangles Date/Map/Set/BigInt/class instances (need custom EJSON/superjson converter); Python needs the opt-in Pydantic converter for datetime; Go silently drops unexported struct fields.

Two-process / four-part mental model (workflow + activity + worker registration + starter) and the `.Get(ctx, &out)` unwrap idiom add friction; testing requires a suite struct with mocks and replay tests to stay safe.
notable_innovations: Automatic, host-language-native serialization via the DataConverter/PayloadConverter chain — zero per-type codec boilerplate in the common case (the exact win Aion's Gleam side lacks); pluggable for encryption/compression codecs and custom formats (MessagePack, Proto).

Replay Testing (WorkflowReplayer / ReplayWorkflowHistory) — run REAL past event histories against new code in CI to catch determinism breaks before deploy; a concrete, tooled answer to the versioning problem worth emulating.
Worker Versioning (pin a whole workflow execution to one code version) as an alternative to per-change GetVersion patching — lets old executions drain on old workers, avoiding branch accumulation.
Deterministic-by-construction replay model with SDK-provided deterministic primitives (workflow.Now, SideEffect, deterministic Sleep/Timer, deterministic goroutines).
Excellent local testing: in-memory TestWorkflowEnvironment with time-skipping and OnActivity() mocking — full workflow tests with no live server.

First-mover 'durable AI agent' story: OpenAI Agents SDK / Vercel AI SDK / Pydantic AI integrations that turn an agent loop into a replayable durable workflow with auto-generated tool schemas from activity signatures.

systems.name: Cadence (Uber)
systems.authoring_model: Cadence is the original Uber engine that Temporal was forked from by the same founding engineers — so the authoring model is nearly identical to Temporal (Temporal ≈ Cadence + refinements). Code-first, primarily Go (go.uber.org/cadence) and Java clients. A workflow is a deterministic Go function taking a Cadence `workflow.Context` first param; activities are ordinary functions; you register workflow+activity on a worker bound to a task list (Cadence's term; Temporal renamed to task queue) and a domain (Cadence's tenancy unit; Temporal renamed to namespace). Same ExecuteActivity/.Get, ActivityOptions, RetryPolicy shapes. Cadence 1.0 was announced (Uber, 2024/25) and it added cross-region/multi-region domain traffic distribution, so it is NOT dead, but new adopters overwhelmingly pick Temporal.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Same automatic DataConverter model as Temporal (Cadence predates and seeded it): a pluggable DataConverter serializes workflow/activity inputs/outputs, default is JSON via Go reflection, source of truth is your native Go/Java types — no hand-written codecs. Same caveats (unexported fields dropped, need json tags; custom converter for non-JSON types/encryption). Effectively zero per-type serialization boilerplate, same strength and same language-native gotchas as Temporal.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Identical model to Temporal: event-sourcing + replay of the workflow function to reconstruct state, so workflow code MUST be deterministic (use Cadence APIs for time, random, threads; no direct IO). Versioning uses the same `workflow.GetVersion` primitive (Go + Java) with the same downside — branch accumulation makes code 'difficult to read and maintain.' Cadence added a newer versioning API separating code change from activation (analogous to Temporal's ExecuteWithVersion), and a 2025 'safe deployment of versioned workflows' guide. Same NonDeterministicException class of production pain.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Essentially the same ceremony profile as Temporal — workflow fn + activity fns + worker registration on a task list + domain + starter client; ActivityOptions/RetryPolicy plumbing and .Get() unwrap per activity. Serialization boilerplate ~zero (host-language reflection). If anything slightly more raw/less polished than Temporal's SDKs and with fewer languages (no first-class TS/Python parity), so the ergonomic ceiling is lower.
systems.visual_editor: No first-party visual authoring. Cadence ships a Web UI for viewing/searching workflow executions and history (operational, like Temporal's), not for authoring. Code-only authoring.
systems.agent_support: No first-party AI-agent / durable-LLM story. Cadence has not pursued the 'durable AI agent' positioning that Temporal is investing in (no OpenAI Agents SDK / Vercel AI SDK / Pydantic integrations). Agent workflows would just be hand-written durable workflows with generic UI observability.
top_pain_points: Same determinism/replay and GetVersion branch-accumulation maintenance pain as Temporal (shared root; the canonical 'maintenance challenges of Temporal/Cadence workflow versioning' write-ups cover both).

Declining community momentum / effectively maintenance-adjacent: 2024-25 open-source-ecosystem analyses flag Cadence in a 'danger zone' with low PR/community activity versus Temporal; most new projects choose Temporal for the more active community and support.
Fewer/less-polished SDKs than Temporal (strong Go, some Java; no first-class TypeScript/Python parity), so worse authoring ergonomics for non-Go teams.
Same heavy self-hosting footprint (Cassandra/DB + services) and distributed-systems learning curve as Temporal.
No AI-agent tooling and no visual authoring — trails Temporal on the two newest fronts.
notable_innovations: Originated the entire durable-execution/replay + automatic-DataConverter model that Temporal now popularizes (the foundational design lineage).
Cadence 1.0 (Uber, 2024/25) added multi-region domain support and traffic distribution across regions — active work on geo-distribution/HA at Uber scale.
Still production-critical at very large scale (1000+ services at Uber; used by DoorDash, HashiCorp, Coinbase historically) — proven durability at scale.
Newer versioning API separating change-authoring from activation, plus 2025 safe-deployment guidance for versioned workflows.

findings.cluster: Restate + DBOS (next-generation "lightweight" durable-execution systems that pitch lower ceremony than Temporal)

systems.name: Restate
systems.authoring_model: Code-first, HTTP-native durable execution. SDKs in TypeScript, Java/Kotlin, Rust, Go, Python. Three core abstractions: (1) Services (stateless handlers), (2) Virtual Objects (keyed, single-writer stateful entities with per-key durable K/V state and serialized concurrency), (3) Workflows (a Virtual Object with a mandatory `run` handler that executes exactly once per key). A minimal TS workflow is literally: `export const signupWorkflow = restate.workflow({ name: "SignupWorkflow", handlers: { run: async (ctx: WorkflowContext, user: User) => { const id = await ctx.run("create", () => createUser(user)); ctx.set("status", "created"); return id; } } });` The typed input/output are just the handler's function signature. Durable side-effects are wrapped in `ctx.run("label", () => ...)`. State is `ctx.get/ctx.set`. Handlers are invokable over plain HTTP/gRPC — no separate 'activity worker' registration ceremony; the durable orchestration and the effectful work live in the same handler code, with `ctx.run` marking the durable boundary inline. Awakeables (`ctx.awakeable()`) give durable promises for human-in-the-loop / external signals.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Automatic by default — this is a key strength vs Aion's Gleam. TS SDK uses built-in JSON (structural typing: you declare `user: User` in the signature and it just works, no hand-written codec). Java defaults to Jackson Databind (reflection), Kotlin to kotlinx.serialization, Rust to serde. No per-type codec is hand-written for the common case. Source of truth = your language's native types. You CAN opt into schema validation/coercion via any Standard-Schema library (e.g. Zod) for handler input/output, and Restate generates JSON Schema/OpenAPI from those (Zod for TS; also Go/Java/Kotlin/Python). Custom serialization is available via a `Serde` interface (e.g. `restate.serde.binary` for bytes, or per-call `serde` option on `ctx.run/ctx.get/ctx.set/ctx.awakeable`) but is opt-in, not required. Net: near-zero serialization boilerplate; the tax Aion's Gleam pays does not exist here because these host languages have reflective/derive-based serialization.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Journal-based (log/replay) durable execution — same family as Temporal, so the AUTHOR must still think about determinism. Any non-deterministic operation (I/O, randomness, time, API calls) MUST be wrapped in `ctx.run(...)` or a context primitive, or replay produces wrong results / non-determinism errors. The `ctx.run` boundary is explicit and author-visible (unlike DBOS where a whole function is a step). Recent versions moved to an append-only journal where commands and completions are separate entries. Versioning is a notable pain: executions are pinned to immutable deployment URLs/versions; to change code you must run old and new endpoints side-by-side and drain in-flight invocations on the old version — you cannot hot-edit code for a workflow that has already journaled steps without risking non-determinism failures. Determinism enforcement is at runtime, described by one analyst as 'the most explicit idempotency and compensation semantics of any platform.'
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Very low ceremony relative to Temporal and dramatically lower than Aion's current Gleam experience. A minimal workflow is essentially your typed handler signature + your order-of-operations, with `ctx.run("label", ...)` sprinkled at durable boundaries. No separate worker-registration file, no input/output/error codec, no Dynamic-payload unwrap entrypoint. The main 'glue' that remains is (a) the `ctx.run` labels and (b) understanding which calls must go through the context. Intent-to-glue ratio is high (mostly intent). The residual cognitive tax is the replay/determinism mental model, not textual boilerplate.
systems.visual_editor: No visual/graph AUTHORING editor — Restate is deliberately code-first and has published arguments against graph/diagram authoring. It DOES ship a built-in web UI (the Restate UI / console) that auto-provides observability with zero setup: it lists all invocations, shows the per-invocation journal (each command/step, intermediate results, state), and surfaces agent execution steps. It is observe/operate-oriented (introspect, cancel, manage invocations), not a diagram round-trip designer.
systems.agent_support: Strong and explicitly marketed as a first-class use case. Integrates with agent SDKs (Vercel AI SDK, OpenAI Agents SDK, Google ADK, Pydantic AI, LangChain/LangGraph) by wrapping LLM/tool calls as durable steps WITHOUT SDK-specific rewrites — 'agents are just code' (functions + loops), mixing deterministic and agentic sections freely. Awakeables give durable human-in-the-loop approvals ('pay for active work, not wait time'). Virtual Objects give per-session keyed conversation state + serialized concurrency for multi-turn. Reliable messaging + end-to-end idempotency for multi-agent hand-offs. The UI shows agent journals for live observability. A dedicated restatedev/ai-examples repo covers agents, A2A, MCP. This overlaps directly with Aion's NOI thesis — Restate offers observability of agent steps but its intervention story is thinner (cancel/manage) vs Aion's inject-message/mid-run intervention.
top_pain_points: Versioning is dangerous/awkward: executions pinned to immutable deployment URLs; you must run old+new endpoints side-by-side and drain in-flight work — you cannot safely edit code that has already journaled steps (non-determinism errors otherwise).

Determinism learning curve is a real adoption barrier — Restate itself blogged that the catch-22 is nobody wants to learn deep replay semantics just to adopt a new tool; devs must understand why time/random/IO must go through the context.
Smaller production ecosystem and deployment base than Temporal; thinner community and fewer third-party integrations (analyst gave high Vision but lower Ability-to-Execute).
Requires running the Restate server/runtime as a broker between callers and handlers (it is stateful infra in the request path), so it is not purely 'a library in your process' the way DBOS pitches.
Idempotency/compensation (saga) burden for external side-effects still falls on the author — durability doesn't remove the need to make steps safe to retry.
notable_innovations: Virtual Objects: keyed single-writer entities with durable per-key K/V state AND serialized concurrency — one primitive that unifies actor-style state, mutual exclusion, and workflow, eliminating a lot of locking boilerplate (highlighted in Restate 1.3 'concurrency without losing sleep').

HTTP-native / deploy-like-your-app: handlers are ordinary HTTP endpoints; no separate orchestrator+worker split to stand up — durable orchestration and effectful code co-locate, with `ctx.run` marking the durable boundary inline.
Awakeables — durable promises as a clean, uniform primitive for human-in-the-loop, external callbacks, and long waits without paying for idle compute.
Automatic reflective/schema serialization with optional Standard-Schema (Zod) validation that ALSO generates OpenAPI/JSON Schema — one source of truth (the type) drives runtime + docs.
Framework-agnostic agent durability: wrap existing agent SDKs without adopting a proprietary graph — a strong counter-position to LangGraph-style graph authoring.

systems.name: DBOS (DBOS Transact)
systems.authoring_model: 'Durability as a library' — an open-source library you import into your existing app, backed by Postgres, NOT external orchestrator infra. SDKs in TypeScript and Python (Java/Go emerging). Core abstraction = lightweight decorators/wrappers on ORDINARY functions: `@DBOS.workflow()` marks orchestration, `@DBOS.step()` marks a durable step. A minimal TS example is literally: `class Example { @DBOS.step() static async stepOne(){...} @DBOS.step() static async stepTwo(){...} @DBOS.workflow() static async exampleWorkflow(){ await Example.stepOne(); await Example.stepTwo(); } }`. Non-decorator registration also exists: `DBOS.registerWorkflow(fn)` + `DBOS.runStep(() => fn(), {name})`. Python is identical with `@DBOS.workflow()` / `@DBOS.step()`. There is essentially no entrypoint ceremony — a workflow is a normal function that calls normal (decorated) functions in order. It runs in-process; no separate worker binary and no broker in the request path.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Automatic — 'inputs and outputs must be serializable to JSON' and the library handles encoding/decoding transparently; authors do NOT hand-write codecs. Source of truth = the language's native types (TS types / Python type hints). Optional `serialization: "portable"` mode plus an input schema (e.g. Pydantic/zod) enables validation + coercion (e.g. dates) and cross-language interop (a Python/Java app reading events set by a TS app) via a `serializationType` parameter on some methods. Default 'native' serialization is per-language. The only friction devs raise is that JSON-only excludes easy binary values (a commenter requested CBOR). Overall the per-type boilerplate that Aion's Gleam suffers is absent because JS/Python have reflective JSON serialization.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability is largely INVISIBLE at the code level — the framework checkpoints each step's completion to Postgres tables and, on crash, automatically resumes from the last completed step. You don't write persistence code; you just decorate. Determinism model: the WORKFLOW function must be deterministic (its control flow), but STEPS may be non-deterministic (LLM calls, randomness, IO) — anything non-deterministic must live inside a `@DBOS.step()`. This split confused HN readers (determinism preached but LLMs are non-deterministic) until clarified; docs could front-load it. Versioning is a real limitation: the assumed model is 'every workflow finishes on the code version it started'; changing code for an already-advanced running workflow is 'not recommended', and better tooling for retroaction is acknowledged-but-not-built. Because all state lives in plain Postgres tables, custom recovery/migration is 'doable' by hand.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Lowest textual ceremony of any system studied — arguably the ideal 'just your types + order-of-operations'. A minimal workflow is two decorators and normal function calls; no worker process, no codecs, no Dynamic unwrap entrypoint, no separate orchestrator to boot. Nearly all lines are intent. Residual glue = (a) deciding step boundaries, (b) keeping the workflow body deterministic, (c) writing saga/compensation logic yourself for external side effects. Some devs find decorators feel mandatory/restrictive and asked for a decorator-free path (which exists via registerWorkflow). This is the closest existing model to Aion's stated authoring goal.
systems.visual_editor: No visual authoring/graph designer. It ships DBOS Conductor — an ops control center (DBOS-hosted OR self-hostable/air-gapped) that gives dashboards of active/past/queued workflows, real-time workflow visualization including the execution GRAPH (workflow -> steps -> child workflows, showing fan-out/fan-in), filtering by time/id/name/status, and lifecycle control: pause, resume, cancel, restart-from-a-specific-step, fork. Also emits OpenTelemetry spans and OpenMetrics for external observability tooling. A community open-source read-only viewer (dbos-argus) exists too. So visualization is observe/operate-first, not code<->diagram round-trip authoring.
systems.agent_support: First-class and heavily marketed for 'crashproof AI agents'. Any agent can be wrapped in a DBOSAgent which auto-wraps Agent.run / run_sync as DBOS workflows and wraps model requests + MCP communication as DBOS steps — so each LLM/tool call is durably checkpointed and resumes after crash without lost progress. Official integrations with LangGraph and Pydantic AI (documented). Observability: because every step is checkpointed in Postgres, Conductor visualizes agent progress in real time; DBOS + Pydantic AI OTel spans compose into one trace from high-level agent reasoning down to individual retries/checkpointed steps. Human-in-the-loop and long waits are supported via durable workflow suspension. Overlaps Aion NOI on observability; intervention is present (pause/cancel/fork/restart-from-step) though not a live inject-message-into-transcript model.
top_pain_points: Postgres is a hard dependency and a potential bottleneck/scaling ceiling — a former Temporal engineer pressed on load; DBOS conceded ~10K+ steps/sec on a big DB before you must shard/split services. Devs asked to genericize storage (e.g. RocksDB) but it's Postgres-coupled.
JSON-only serialization for step args/returns — no easy binary (CBOR requested); complex/binary data types are awkward.
Versioning/code-evolution of in-flight workflows is 'not recommended'; the model assumes a workflow completes on the version it started, and retroaction tooling is acknowledged as missing.
Determinism split (deterministic workflow vs non-deterministic steps) is under-explained and initially confusing, especially juxtaposed with LLM use cases.
Idempotency / saga compensation for external side-effects is still the author's burden — a step that touched multiple systems before failing needs hand-written backup/compensation steps.
Smaller polish/ecosystem signals — devs noted broken docs buttons and asked for distributed-setup examples; decorator syntax felt mandatory to some (non-decorator API exists but is less advertised).
notable_innovations: 'Durability as a library' in-process model: no separate orchestrator server + worker servers to stand up, load, and submit to — this removes an entire class of external-orchestration friction (the exact friction Temporal-style systems impose). Lowest operational + textual ceremony of the cohort.

Invisible durability via two decorators — the author writes ordinary functions; checkpointing to Postgres is transparent. Closest existing realization of Aion's 'workflow = just your types + order of operations' goal.
State lives in plain, queryable Postgres tables — you can inspect/operate on durable state with SQL, and custom migration/retroaction is possible directly on the DB (transparency as a feature).
DBOSAgent auto-wrapping: turn an existing agent into a durable workflow with a one-line wrap that maps model/tool/MCP calls to durable steps — very low-friction agent durability.

Conductor + OTel/OpenMetrics: real-time execution-graph visualization (fan-out/fan-in) plus lifecycle ops (pause/resume/cancel/fork/restart-from-step) that is self-hostable/air-gapped — a strong ops-console reference for Aion's ops-console ambitions.
Portable serialization mode with schema-driven validation/coercion enabling cross-language (TS<->Python<->Java) interop over the same Postgres state.

findings.cluster: Inngest + Trigger.dev — TypeScript-first, DX-polished durable/step-function systems

systems.name: Inngest
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Authors write essentially NO codecs — this is the sharpest contrast with the Gleam/Aion model. Everything crossing the boundary (event payloads, step return values, invoke args) is plain JSON-serializable TS; the SDK JSON-serializes/deserializes automatically via structural typing. Type safety is opt-in and *type-only by default*: authors declare event shapes and get `event.data` typed end-to-end. In v3 this was centralized: `new EventSchemas().fromRecord<{ 'app/user.created': { data: { userId: string } } }>()` (or `.fromZod(...)` / `.fromUnion<...>()`), passed as `schemas` to the client, with `GetEvents<typeof inngest>` to extract types. In v4 (2025) this was replaced by decentralized `eventType("user/created", { schema: z.object({...}) })` co-located with the function, or `staticSchema<T>()` for compile-time-only typing with no runtime validation and no schema library. Runtime validation is NOT automatic — it requires `@inngest/middleware-validation` + a Zod/Standard-Schema schema. So the source of truth is your TS types (optionally mirrored by a Zod schema if you want runtime checks); there is no proto/IDL and no per-type hand-written encoder/decoder. The 'codec tax' Aion pays in Gleam is essentially free here because TS is structurally typed and JSON-native.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability is via *step memoization / replay*, but the determinism burden on the author is much lower than Temporal-style replay because the ORCHESTRATION layer has 'no determinism requirement' — every non-deterministic thing (LLM call, DB, API, `Date.now`, random) is naturally a step, and Inngest markets this as ideal for AI (no replay model to design LLM calls around). Mechanically: on each step boundary the SDK re-invokes the entire function; `step.run(id, ...)` checks if that id's result is already persisted and returns the cached value instead of re-executing. THE key author-facing gotcha (repeatedly cited): code OUTSIDE `step.run()` runs on every re-invocation, so any side effect between steps executes N times for N steps ('you'll log 4 times'). Authors must defensively wrap all side effects in steps, and step IDs must be stable/unique. v4 added checkpointing-by-default so steps can execute eagerly within one request, cutting the per-step HTTP round-trips that caused latency. Durability state is keyed by function+run+step-id. Versioning: in-flight runs continue on old logic; changing step structure of live runs is the usual replay-versioning hazard, though less severe than Temporal because there's no full-history replay of arbitrary workflow-body code.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Very low ceremony. A minimal function is ~8 lines: client + `createFunction({id}, {event}, async ({event, step}) => {...})`. The 'intent' (order of operations + which calls are durable) dominates; glue is basically the `{ id }` object and the `step.run("id", ...)` wrapper around each side effect. Compared to Aion's Gleam ~27-line unwrap/decode entrypoint + ~20-line hand-written codec per type, Inngest is dramatically leaner: zero codec code, zero manual payload unwrapping, no separate worker registration. The only recurring tax is (a) inventing a stable string id per step and (b) discipline to keep side effects inside steps. Optional event schemas add a few lines per event type if you want runtime validation, but the common path is 'just your types + your order of operations,' which is close to Aion's stated goal.
systems.visual_editor: No visual authoring/designer — it is strictly code-first (a selling point vs AWS Step Functions' JSON ASL / Workflow Studio, which the DEV.to comparison author explicitly preferred to avoid). What exists is strong observability, NOT editing: a cloud dashboard + local Dev Server (`npx inngest-cli dev`) with a function/run inspector, step-by-step execution view, OpenTelemetry-powered distributed tracing ('Extended Traces' capturing external API/DB calls beyond built-in steps), and run replay. So: code-first authoring, rich visual *debugging/observability*, no drag-and-drop workflow builder and no diagram<->code round-tripping.
systems.agent_support: First-class and a headline focus. AgentKit (`@inngest/agent-kit`) is a TS multi-agent framework: `createAgent({ name, system, model, tools })`, `createTool({ name, parameters: z.object(...), handler })`, and `createNetwork({ agents, router, state })` where a code-based or LLM router picks the next agent based on shared `network.state`. It runs on the Inngest engine so each agent/LLM call is a durable step (fault-tolerant, retriable, memoized to avoid re-spending tokens) and is observable in the same dev server / trace UI. Embraces MCP for tooling and multiple model providers (Anthropic, etc.). The step model is pitched as inherently agent-friendly because LLM non-determinism needs no special replay isolation. What's less emphasized vs Aion's NOI: mid-run human intervention (inject-message/cancel) — Inngest has `step.waitForEvent`/`waitForSignal`-style pausing and cancellation events, but there isn't the same live-transcript-streaming + operator-intervention ops-console framing.
top_pain_points: Side-effects outside step.run() re-execute on every function re-invocation — the #1 real-world gotcha; devs get surprise duplicate logs/writes and must defensively wrap everything in steps (DEV.to week-long comparison; docs; multiple guides).

Step model breaks long single operations into discrete HTTP requests, each bounded by the host platform's timeout — 'struggles with tasks that need to run for extended periods as a single unbroken execution' (2026 comparison articles).

Type safety is only as good as the discipline of defining event schemas up front; default typing is compile-time-only and runtime validation is not automatic (needs @inngest/middleware-validation + Zod).
v4 was a breaking release: EventSchemas removed in favor of eventType()/staticSchema(); v3 middleware 'not forward-compatible' and must be rewritten — real migration friction for existing apps.

Local Dev Server friction reported in GitHub issues: HTTPS URLs not supported (falls back to HTTP POST and steps fail #433), Docker dev server sync failures (#1882), ETIMEDOUT timeouts (#1568), events failing when devserver isn't running (#271), npx cache corruption requiring `rm -rf ~/.npm/_npx`.

Cost scaling concerns at volume vs raw queues (DEV.to author: ~$80/mo for 1k articles vs ~$5 on Step Functions); one team (Monk) migrated 100+ workflows off Inngest citing vague 'hurt us at scale', and comparisons note smaller ecosystem/fewer native integrations than AWS.
notable_innovations: Event-driven + step model that removes the Temporal-style determinism burden from the orchestration layer — LLM/AI calls are 'naturally a step' with no replay isolation to design, a genuine authoring simplification Aion could learn from.

Codec-free authoring: structural TS + JSON-native means zero hand-written serialization; optional layered schema typing (fromRecord/fromZod/fromUnion, now eventType/staticSchema) lets authors pay for validation only where wanted.
Steps mounted inside the user's existing HTTP app (Next.js/Express) — no separate worker binary to run/register, collapsing the workflow/activity process split that Aion currently has.
Per-tenant/per-org concurrency & flow control ('virtual queue per org, never more than N steps at a time') — cited as a feature Temporal couldn't easily match; strong multi-tenant primitive.
AgentKit: durable multi-agent networks with deterministic code-routers OR LLM-routers over shared State, MCP-native, each agent call a durable retriable step — a clean model for durable agents.
v4 eager checkpointing + default parallel-step optimization to cut per-step HTTP round-trips (near-zero inter-step latency).

systems.name: Trigger.dev (v3/v4)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Authors write NO codecs. Payloads and return values are plain TS objects, serialized automatically with **superjson** (a JSON superset) — so Dates, BigInt, Map/Set, etc. survive the boundary as native JS types (pass a `Date` directly, don't `JSON.stringify` it). Source of truth is your TS types. For runtime validation you opt into `schemaTask({ schema: z.object({...}), run })` (Standard Schema / Zod / Yup etc.), which makes the payload BOTH typed and runtime-validated. Payload size rules are handled transparently: >512KB is offloaded to S3-compatible object storage with a URL stored in the DB; hard cap ~10MB. So the per-type boilerplate is zero (or a single Zod schema if you want validation) — again a stark contrast to Aion's Gleam hand-written to_json/decoder per type.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability is via **process checkpoint/restore (CRIU)** — NOT replay/memoization. When a task hits a wait (`wait.for`, `triggerAndWait`, a waitpoint token) the system checkpoints the ENTIRE process state (memory, CPU registers, open FDs), compresses/stores it, and restores it when the wait resolves — so you don't pay for idle time and there is effectively NO determinism/replay burden on the author: the code between waits does not get re-executed, so `Date.now()`/random/side-effects between waits are safe (the opposite of Inngest's #1 gotcha). Idempotency keys give result caching for retried child tasks so only failed tasks + downstream retry. Versioning is a genuine strength: 'atomic versioning' — a new deploy does not affect runs already in-flight; each run pins to its deployed version. Author mental model is close to 'just write normal long-running async code'; the main things they must remember are `await` on trigger calls, unwrapping the returned `Result` (`.ok`/`.output`/`.unwrap()`), and not wrapping `triggerAndWait` in `Promise.all` (use batch APIs).
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Low ceremony, arguably even lower conceptual load than Inngest because there's no step-id discipline and no 'keep side effects in steps' rule (CRIU checkpointing removes replay concerns). Minimal task ~6-8 lines: `task({ id, run: async (payload) => {...} })`. The 'intent' is the `run` body; glue is the `{ id }`, the named-export requirement, and per-call `await`/Result-unwrapping. vs Aion's Gleam: no entrypoint decode ceremony, no per-type codecs, but you do accept a separate managed/self-hosted runtime rather than everything in-process. schemaTask adds one Zod schema per validated task if desired. Overall very close to 'your types + your order of operations'.
systems.visual_editor: No visual/drag-and-drop workflow authoring — code-first. But the observability/DX surface is a major selling point and richer on the *frontend-integration* axis than Inngest: a cloud dashboard with OpenTelemetry-powered traces/logs (auto-instruments Prisma, AWS SDK, etc.), full run history, run inspection, and replay. Crucially it has **Realtime** (GA): server->frontend streaming of run status/output via `useRealtimeRun` / `useRealtimeRunsWithTag` React hooks, enabling live dashboards and building your OWN workflow/agent UIs. Their human-in-the-loop example even composes ReactFlow + waitpoint tokens into an approval UI — but that's user-built, not a first-party visual editor. So: code-first authoring, strong live-observability + first-class realtime hooks for building custom UIs, no built-in diagram<->code designer.
systems.agent_support: Strong and explicitly positioned as an AI-agent/workflow platform ('build and deploy fully-managed AI agents and workflows'). No branded multi-agent framework like Inngest's AgentKit, but the primitives are very agent-oriented: durable long-running tasks (LLM chains without timeout via checkpointing), **waitpoint tokens** (`wait.createToken()` → complete/timeout) that are the canonical human-in-the-loop / approval-gate primitive (reject/suggest/approve mid-run), **Realtime** hooks to stream agent run progress/output to a frontend live, and an agent toolkit + MCP/agent-skills on the roadmap. This maps closely to Aion's NOI goals: waitpoints ≈ mid-run intervention, Realtime ≈ live transcript streaming. Cold-start work (MicroVMs, <500ms) is aimed at making agents responsive.
top_pain_points: Result-object footguns: `triggerAndWait` returns a `Result` ({ ok, output }); forgetting to `.unwrap()`/check `.ok` silently mishandles failures — a top listed mistake.

Concurrency anti-pattern: wrapping `triggerAndWait` in `Promise.all` is unsupported/breaks — must use built-in batch APIs; and forgetting `await` on trigger calls lets the process exit before the task runs.
Named-export requirement: tasks that aren't named exports silently fail to deploy — a common setup trap.
v3→v4 migration friction: import path changed (`@trigger.dev/sdk/v3` → `@trigger.dev/sdk`), deprecated v2 `client.defineJob()` APIs, evolving type system/APIs ('newer framework, still evolving').
onFailure gaps cause DB inconsistency when `maxDuration` is exceeded; devs must add explicit failure handlers.

Historically slow cold starts and self-hosting/infra weight (managed runtime + CRIU + object store), which they are actively fixing via MicroVMs (<500ms) and concurrency/pricing changes in late 2025 — implying these were real prior friction points.
notable_innovations: CRIU process checkpoint/restore for durability — no replay, no determinism burden, no step-id ceremony; code between waits simply resumes. This is a fundamentally different (and author-friendlier) durability model than memoization/replay and directly eliminates Inngest's #1 gotcha.
superjson serialization: Dates/BigInt/Map/Set cross the boundary as native JS types with zero author effort — richer than plain-JSON and far beyond Aion's hand-written Gleam codecs.
Transparent large-payload offload: >512KB auto-uploaded to S3-compatible storage, URL stored in DB (10MB cap) — author never thinks about it.
Atomic versioning: in-flight runs pinned to their deploy version, new deploys don't disturb them — a clean answer to the workflow-versioning pain that plagues replay systems.
Realtime + React hooks (useRealtimeRun / useRealtimeRunsWithTag) for live server->frontend streaming of run state/output — strong primitive for building custom agent/ops UIs (parallels Aion NOI).
Waitpoint tokens as a general blocking primitive (tags/timeout/idempotency) — clean, composable human-in-the-loop and cross-run synchronization.
New v4 Run Engine (20k run updates/sec) and OpenTelemetry-native tracing with auto-instrumentation of common libraries.

findings.cluster: Transparent/Invisible Durability: Golem Cloud (WASM-snapshot durable execution) + Azure Durable Functions (deterministic orchestrator + replay). Two ends of the "transparency" spectrum: Golem makes durability nearly invisible at the language level (the whole program is transparently durable via oplog-of-WASI-effects), trading it for a heavy WASM component-model substrate; Durable Functions makes durability an explicit mental model (deterministic orchestrator, await = checkpoint, no I/O in orchestrator) but with familiar C#/JS/Python and mature tooling. The key axis for Aion: Golem proves you CAN get automatic serialization + zero-annotation durability if you own the runtime; Durable Functions proves that even with a "just await" model, replay-determinism leaks constantly into the author's face.

systems.name: Golem Cloud (durable agent runtime, WASM)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: This is Golem's headline strength and the sharpest contrast with Aion's Gleam codec tax. Serialization is AUTOMATIC and derived from types — the author writes essentially zero codec code. Rust: types crossing agent boundaries derive a `Schema` trait (one derive line: `#[derive(..., Schema)]`), mapped through WIT (the WASM Component Model IDL, Protobuf-like). TypeScript: a build-time tool `golem-typegen` uses the TypeScript compiler API to EXTRACT type info directly from source and generate the WIT schema automatically — 'we can define and use complex custom data types in the agent's interface without having to manually write any schema for them.' The single source of truth is the language's own types; WIT is generated, not hand-authored. Golem markets exactly the pain Aion feels: 'State changes and effects are captured automatically, without serialization, state machines, or annotations' and activities 'are stored not as blobs ... but as fully typed data structures.' The cost is moved into the toolchain/runtime (WASM component model, WIT, typegen), not onto the author.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Radically transparent compared to Durable Functions. Durability = an append-only oplog that records every interaction the WASM program has with the outside world (all WASI calls tracked) plus incremental state snapshots (tiny deltas, NOT whole-memory snapshots, to keep latency low). On crash/node-loss the worker is reconstructed on another machine by replaying its own oplog 'at the exact millisecond it left off.' Crucially the AUTHOR mostly does NOT reason about determinism: because ALL nondeterministic effects (time, random, network, LLM responses) go through WASI/host interfaces, they are captured in the oplog and replayed as recorded — so ordinary imperative code that calls the clock or a random source is durable-safe without special APIs. This is the inverse of Durable Functions' 'never call DateTime.Now' rule. Durable LLM providers (e.g. DurableOpenAI) record responses in the oplog and replay them on recovery. Idle agents suspend-to-zero (no memory) and resume deterministically. Versioning of running workers exists ('Updating Agents') but is the weak spot — see pain points; oplog replay across code changes is inherently fragile, and large payloads are offloaded to blob storage with only references kept inline to bound oplog growth.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Among the lowest ceremony of any durable system surveyed — arguably the target Aion is chasing. A minimal agent is ~6-10 lines of TS that are ALL intent (state field + method), with the only 'glue' being `extends BaseAgent` + `@agent()`. No entrypoint ceremony, no input/output/error codec, no explicit step/activity wrapping, no replay guards. The ceremony that DOES exist is structural/one-time: understanding the agent model, and (if you drop to Rust/WIT) the WASM Component Model tooling. Compared to Aion's ~27-line Gleam entrypoint + ~20-lines-per-type codec tax, Golem effectively eliminates both categories: the run-ceremony (no orchestrator boilerplate) and the codec tax (schema derived from types).
systems.visual_editor: No visual workflow/graph EDITOR — it is strictly code-first. There is a 'Console' GUI for managing accounts, components, and workers, but it is tied to the hosted Cloud offering and 'relies on APIs that don't even exist in OSS or make sense there' (open issue #1189 requests an OSS TS GUI). Rib is a small scripting language + REPL (WAVE/WASM value syntax) for defining HTTP API gateways on top of components and poking workers interactively — closer to a config/query DSL than a designer. No code<->diagram round-tripping. Observability of running workers exists but there is no LangGraph-Studio-style live graph debugger.
systems.agent_support: First-class and now the primary positioning (rebranded May 2025 as 'the durable agent runtime' / 'agent-native platform'). Agents ARE the core primitive. `golem-ai-llm` gives a unified chat-completion API (OpenAI/Anthropic/etc.) with blocking + streaming, multi-turn, tool-calling, multimodal; Durable provider types record LLM responses in the oplog and replay on recovery — so an agent's LLM calls are exactly-once and crash-survivable for free. Exactly-once tool-call semantics and automatic state persistence across sessions are built in. Weaker on the live-observability/human-intervention plane that Aion's NOI targets: no strong evidence of a first-class live-transcript-streaming ops console or mid-run inject-message/intervention UI comparable to NOI (this is a genuine gap Aion can beat).
top_pain_points: WASM ecosystem immaturity: 'WASM is relatively messy and immature'; you 'can't use any code that relies on threads,' so every dependency must be audited for thread usage — a hard constraint that silently rules out large swaths of existing libraries (HN).
Runtime maturity / preview status: Cloud service is still Developer Preview with paid GA + formal SLAs and data-retention guarantees only planned for Q3 2026 — not yet a de-risked production bet.

Memory/RAM economics questioned: because each agent is a live (though suspendable) WASM instance, HN commenters worried whether RAM usage and pricing are competitive vs databases that already page cold data to disk; 'you will still have to think about managing memory.'
Recovery latency: oplog replay to reconstruct a worker on another node (and reloading passivated/suspended agents) introduces latency — durability is not free at recovery time.

Versioning of long-running workers is the classic transparent-durability hazard: oplog replay across code changes is inherently fragile; the docs gesture at 'Updating Agents' but this is underspecified relative to how central it is.

Learning-curve cliff if you leave the happy path: TS/Rust SDKs hide it, but Rib, WIT, WAVE, and the Component Model surface quickly for API gateways or cross-language work — Golem itself admits 'developers ... don't want to have to learn about ... WIT, or any other WASM-specific tooling.'
notable_innovations: Truly transparent durability: ALL nondeterministic effects captured at the WASI boundary and replayed, so ordinary imperative code (clock, random, network, LLM) is durable-safe WITHOUT special APIs or annotations — the inverse of Durable Functions' constraint list. This is the single biggest thing Aion should study.

Automatic type-derived serialization with the LANGUAGE as source of truth: Rust `#[derive(Schema)]` and especially TS `golem-typegen` extracting schema straight from the compiler — directly solves Aion's codec tax (types described once, not thrice).

Incremental delta snapshots (not whole-memory) to keep durability low-latency; layered oplog storage (Redis-hot -> compressed -> blob-cold) with large payloads offloaded to blob and referenced inline to bound growth.
Suspend-to-zero agents (idle = 0 memory, resumed deterministically by replaying own oplog) — a clean durable-object economic model.
Exactly-once agent-to-agent calls and exactly-once durable LLM/tool calls as a runtime guarantee, not author-implemented — strong for agent workloads.

systems.name: Azure Durable Functions (orchestrator + replay)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Automatic but JSON-reflection-based, which is a double-edged sword. Inputs/outputs of activities and orchestrator I/O are serialized to/from JSON by the framework (Json.NET / System.Text.Json in .NET) with no hand-written codec — so at the happy path there is far less serialization boilerplate than Aion's Gleam. The source of truth is the language type + its serializer attributes. BUT because (de)serialization happens implicitly during replay, serialization is a documented source of nondeterminism failures: one real root cause was the framework being unable to deserialize an activity's JSON at playback because a needed DLL wasn't loaded when the orchestration was first in a new process — 'technically non-deterministic.' Large inputs/outputs bloat the history table and 'risk causing memory exceptions during replay,' so the author must actively keep payloads small — serialization ergonomics are good until scale/history-size forces manual discipline.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability is achieved by event-sourcing the orchestrator: the Durable Task Framework 'reruns the orchestrator function from scratch' on every resume and consults the execution history to replay completed task outputs. This forces the AUTHOR to think about determinism CONSTANTLY — the opposite of Golem. Documented constraints the author must internalize: no DateTime.Now / current time (use context.CurrentUtcDateTime), no Guid.NewGuid() (use context.NewGuid()), no random, no I/O or network or filesystem in the orchestrator (push it to activities), no non-deterministic collection iteration order, no static/mutable shared state, no infinite/unbounded histories. Versioning is a first-class hazard: deploying breaking changes to a running orchestrator causes 'Non-Deterministic workflow detected' replay failures, stuck-in-Running orchestrations, or perf degradation. Mitigation is heavyweight: side-by-side deploys via deployment slots and draining old instances, or the newer built-in Orchestration Versioning feature (automatic version isolation, backend-agnostic) — but the safe path is still 'let all old-version instances finish before removing old code paths.'
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Moderate. The orchestrator body itself is low-ceremony and reads like normal async code — mostly intent (the order of awaits). But the surrounding structure is heavier than Golem: you split every unit of real work into a separately-declared activity function with its own trigger attribute and signature, plus a client/starter function to kick things off. So the 'glue' is the function-boundary plumbing (attributes, trigger bindings, the orchestrator/activity/client triad) and, more insidiously, the invisible determinism tax — the author must constantly self-police what code is legal inside the orchestrator. Less textual boilerplate than Aion's codec tax, but a larger COGNITIVE boilerplate around determinism.
systems.visual_editor: No visual workflow designer / no code<->diagram round-tripping (it is code-first, unlike Step Functions or Logic Apps). The observability story is now strong, however: the Durable Task Scheduler (DTS) — the recommended managed backend — ships a UI DASHBOARD with real-time monitoring of active orchestrations, queued work items, task timing, conversation history, and multi-agent visualization. So there's a good live OBSERVABILITY/monitoring UI, but not an authoring/design surface.
systems.agent_support: Rapidly becoming first-class via the Durable Task extension for the Microsoft Agent Framework (2025-2026). It brings durable execution to AI agents with: checkpointing + resumption, deterministic agent orchestration, durable Agent Framework workflows, human-in-the-loop waits, reliable streaming, idle-session cleanup, dashboard visibility, and distributed execution across stateless workers. The human-interaction pattern models approval as an external event raced against a durable timer — and on the Flex Consumption plan a workflow waiting 24h for approval costs only seconds of compute (no resources consumed while waiting), a strong economic story for long-lived agents. Live observability via the DTS dashboard (conversation history, agent states). Human-in-the-loop is a race-an-external-event pattern rather than a rich live inject/intervene console like Aion's NOI.
top_pain_points: 'Non-Deterministic workflow detected' errors are the dominant, recurring complaint across GitHub issues/discussions (#2347, #2563, #2617, durabletask-dotnet #153) and Microsoft Q&A — often appearing in production, hard/impossible to reproduce locally, sometimes traced to the HOST/runtime rather than user code, forcing repeated purge-and-restart of orchestrations (~10 times over days in one report).

Determinism constraints are a persistent footgun: forbidden DateTime.Now/Guid.NewGuid/random/IO, JS-must-not-be-async, Python-must-be-generators — violations sometimes throw, but often the function 'just behaves weirdly' and eats hours of debugging.

Versioning / deployment of breaking changes is genuinely dangerous: naive deploys break in-flight orchestrations with nondeterministic errors or wedge them in Running forever; the safe path (side-by-side slots, drain old instances) is operationally heavy.
Replay debugging is unintuitive: the orchestrator runs many times, so naive logging/breakpoints fire repeatedly and mislead; you must reason about replay vs first-execution.

History-table growth: large inputs/outputs and long-running orchestrations grow history unbounded, risking memory exceptions during replay and degrading performance — forces the author to keep payloads small and split work.

Serialization-at-replay fragility: implicit JSON (de)serialization during replay can itself be nondeterministic (e.g. DLL/type not loaded on first-in-process replay), producing spurious non-determinism failures unrelated to user logic.
notable_innovations: The 'await = checkpoint' model: durable workflows written in near-idiomatic host-language async code, no explicit step/persist calls — very approachable for mainstream C#/JS/Python devs (broad ecosystem + tooling maturity Aion/Golem lack).
Automatic JSON serialization of activity/orchestrator I/O via the platform serializer — no hand-written codecs on the happy path (contrast Aion's Gleam codec tax).

Extremely cost-efficient long waits: durable timers + external-event races mean a workflow can wait hours/days/weeks consuming ~zero compute (Flex Consumption) — a strong model for human-in-the-loop and long agent tasks.
Built-in Orchestration Versioning feature (automatic, backend-agnostic version isolation) that directly attacks the deploy-breaks-replay problem — a mature answer to a hazard Aion will also face.
Durable Task Scheduler dashboard: managed backend with real-time orchestration/agent observability (task timing, conversation history, multi-agent view) out of the box.
Human-interaction-as-external-event-raced-with-timeout is a clean, composable primitive worth emulating for NOI-style interventions.

findings.cluster: Declarative + visual-first workflow systems: AWS Step Functions (Amazon States Language DSL + Workflow Studio) and Camunda/Zeebe (BPMN 2.0 + Camunda Modeler / Web Modeler). Both externalize the orchestration into a declarative artifact (JSON DSL / BPMN XML) that is the deployed source of truth, and both pair it with a visual editor. The critical contrast with Aion: in these systems the WORKFLOW is data (a diagram/DSL), not code — side-effecting work lives in separately-authored Lambdas / job workers, exactly like Aion's Gleam-workflow vs Rust-worker split. Their hard-won lessons on round-tripping, git diffing declarative artifacts, and expression-language ergonomics are directly load-bearing for Aion's visual-editor ambitions.

systems.name: AWS Step Functions (Amazon States Language + Workflow Studio)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Data is untyped JSON flowing state-to-state; there is NO schema or type system for the payload — the workflow author manually threads/reshapes JSON. Historically this used JSONPath filter fields (`InputPath`, `Parameters`, `ResultSelector`, `ResultPath`, `OutputPath`) with the `.$` suffix convention to mark a field as an expression (e.g. `"ssn.$": "$.data.ssn"`) — verbose, error-prone, and a top newcomer confusion. Nov 2024 added `QueryLanguage: JSONata` + top-level `Assign`/`Arguments`/`Output`, replacing the `.$` dance with `{% ... %}` expressions and named Variables scoped across states. This eliminated whole classes of throwaway Pass/Lambda states that existed only to reshape JSON (AWS's own example replaced a regex-validation Lambda with an inline JSONata `$match`). But it's still hand-written data-plumbing, not derived from types: no compiler checks that a downstream state's expected fields match an upstream producer. The 256KB per-state payload limit forces large data out to S3 with manual pointer-passing (the 'claim check' pattern).
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability is fully managed and transparent to the author — Standard workflows persist every state transition in an execution event history and resume exactly-once across failures; the author never writes replay/checkpoint code and never reasons about determinism (there is no user replay of arbitrary code — the engine just advances the state machine). The cost is leaked as a hard quota: the 25,000-event execution-history limit. Every Lambda invoke emits ~5 events, so long/high-iteration workflows blow the cap; the documented workaround is Distributed Map (child executions) or splitting workflows. Versioning: state machines have Versions and Aliases; in-flight executions keep running their original definition (no live migration of running executions — you drain/finish old versions). Express workflows trade durability for cheap high-throughput (at-least-once, no full history).
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Low textual ceremony for simple flows, but the ceremony is 'wrong-altitude': you write JSON structural scaffolding (StartAt, per-state Type/Next/End, Retry/Catch blocks) rather than intent. Error handling is especially verbose — Retry and Catch arrays with `ErrorEquals`/`IntervalSeconds`/`MaxAttempts`/`BackoffRate` repeated per state. The data-plumbing (pre-JSONata InputPath/ResultPath chains, or JSONata Assign blocks) is pure glue. A realistic state machine is hundreds-to-thousands of lines of JSON; teams universally generate it from CDK/SAM to avoid hand-authoring, which means the 'source of truth' is actually the IaC code, and the ASL is a compiled artifact — a round-trip tension (see visual editor).
systems.visual_editor: Workflow Studio is a genuine two-way visual editor and the strongest round-trip story in this cluster. Design mode (drag states onto a canvas) and Code mode (edit ASL JSON/YAML) stay in sync live: edit the JSON and the graph updates; drag on the canvas and the JSON updates. YAML is converted to JSON for editing and back on save. Now embeddable locally in VS Code (AWS Toolkit, 2024-2025) with local filesystem sync, so it fits an IaC repo. Key limitation: you CANNOT switch from Code mode to Design/Config mode while the ASL is invalid — the visual view requires valid JSON, so mid-edit you're back to raw JSON. Round-trip is at the ASL layer only — it does NOT round-trip to your CDK/SAM code, so teams that author via IaC either treat Studio as read-only visualization or accept a divergence between generated ASL and hand-tuned ASL. Complex JSONata expressions live in text fields inside the visual node; the canvas shows structure, not the expression logic.
systems.agent_support: No first-class LLM/agent-loop concept. Step Functions orchestrates Bedrock model invocations as ordinary Task/SDK integrations and can drive agentic patterns (loop + tool-call via Choice/Map), and AWS positions it for Bedrock/GenAI orchestration, but there is no built-in live-transcript, human-in-the-loop-intervention, or streaming-observability plane for an agent. Human-in-the-loop is done via the callback pattern (`.waitForTaskToken`) — a task pauses until an external token is returned — which is a durable pause primitive but not an agent-intervention UX. Observability is the execution graph + event history + CloudWatch, not a live agent ops-console.
top_pain_points: ASL is verbose, JSON-first and 'optimized for machines not humans' — hand-writing branching/error-handling is painful; teams escape to CDK/SAM/Terraform, which then diverges from what Workflow Studio shows.

Pre-2024 JSONPath data-plumbing (`.$` notation, InputPath/ResultPath/OutputPath threading) was a notorious newcomer trap and forced throwaway Pass/Lambda states just to reshape JSON; JSONata (Nov 2024) improves it but is opt-in per-state and adds a second expression dialect to learn.
Hard 25,000 execution-history-event quota silently kills long/high-iteration workflows (each Lambda invoke ≈5 events); the only fix is architectural (Distributed Map / split workflows).
256KB per-state payload limit forces manual S3 offload + pointer passing ('claim check') for any non-trivial data.

Testing/debugging is hard: no real local run of the whole machine historically; the TestState API (2024) tests one state at a time with mocks, and reviewing failures means reading raw event history, which is time-consuming for branchy workflows.
No typed data contract between states — a state can produce JSON that a downstream state silently doesn't find; errors surface at runtime, not author time.
Vendor lock-in: ASL + AWS service ARNs are non-portable.
notable_innovations: Best-in-cluster live bidirectional round-trip between visual canvas and DSL text (edit either, both update) — the model Aion's visual editor should match or beat.
~200 direct AWS SDK service integrations mean many 'activities' need NO worker code at all — the DSL calls the service directly (a strong ergonomics win vs always hand-writing a worker handler).
`.waitForTaskToken` callback pattern: a first-class durable 'pause until external signal' primitive (clean human-in-the-loop / async-callback building block).
Distributed Map for massive fan-out (up to 10k concurrent child executions) with the parent history staying small — a clean answer to fan-out that sidesteps the history-event cap.
Fully transparent durability: author never thinks about replay/determinism at all — the ceiling to aspire to on 'invisible durability'.
Named Variables + JSONata (2024) let intermediate results be assigned once and referenced anywhere downstream, killing data-threading boilerplate.

systems.name: Camunda 8 / Zeebe (BPMN 2.0 + Camunda Modeler / Web Modeler)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Process variables are an untyped JSON-like document living in the engine. Crossing the model<->worker boundary: on the Java client `@VariablesAsType MyPojo` deserializes selected variables into a typed POJO (Jackson under the hood), and returning a POJO / `.variables(pojo)` serializes it back — so the WORKER side gets typed, reflection-based (de)serialization roughly comparable to serde/Jackson, low boilerplate. But the MODEL side is untyped: there is no schema for process variables, no compile-time contract between what the model expects and what a worker produces. Input/Output variable mappings and conditions are hand-written FEEL expressions (e.g. `= order.total > 100`) referencing variable names as free-form strings — typos and shape-mismatches fail at runtime. Camunda Forms and Connectors have element templates (JSON descriptors) that give some structure, but there is no single typed source of truth spanning model + worker; the data shape is effectively described twice (FEEL mappings in the BPMN, POJO in the worker) with no cross-check.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability is engine-managed and transparent to the author — Zeebe is an event-sourced, partitioned, replicated distributed engine (RocksDB state + replicated log) that persists process state and guarantees each job is handed to a worker until completed (at-least-once job delivery, so workers must be idempotent). The author writes NO replay/checkpoint/determinism code; there is no user-code replay model at all — the engine advances the token through the BPMN graph. The determinism burden lands on worker idempotency, not on authoring. Versioning is a real operational discipline: deploying a changed BPMN creates a new process-definition version; running instances continue on their original version (no automatic migration), and moving in-flight instances requires an explicit process-instance migration with mapping instructions — a well-known source of operational pain when a model changes shape.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Split personality. The happy path (drag a few tasks and gateways, wire connectors) is genuinely low-ceremony and readable by non-developers — the diagram IS the intent for coarse control flow. But real logic pushes ceremony into three places: (1) FEEL expressions buried in element property panels (input/output mappings, gateway conditions) that are invisible on the canvas; (2) a Java/Node worker per job type with its own project, deps, and lifecycle; (3) operational scaffolding (running Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist, or SaaS). So a 'minimal' process is a small diagram PLUS a worker service PLUS a cluster. The glue-vs-intent ratio is good for orchestration shape, poor once branching/data-mapping logic grows — that logic hides in FEEL fields and worker code, defeating the 'the diagram tells the whole story' promise.
systems.visual_editor: The most mature visual-first tooling in the industry: Camunda Modeler (desktop, open-source, Electron) and Web Modeler (collaborative, in Camunda 8 SaaS/SM). BPMN is a diagram-first standard so there is no separate 'code view' to round-trip — the XML is generated FROM the diagram and is not meant to be hand-edited (editing XML by hand is discouraged and can desync layout). Web Modeler adds visual version diffing and (Aug 2024) a changelist feature; 8.8 adds FEEL Copilot. THE core weakness is git: the .bpmn XML interleaves logic and layout (DI coordinates/waypoints), so a trivial name change or nudging a box produces enormous XML churn — one documented case turned a single logical edit into a 257-line diff (82-/86+). This makes PR review and merge-conflict resolution genuinely painful; teams resort to custom git diff-filters (e.g. bpmn-diff-filter strips layout to shrink 257 lines to 9) or Web Modeler's own visual diff. Merge conflicts in feature-branch workflows often can't be auto-resolved and force re-applying changes by hand. Native GitHub sync (2024) exists but was initially manual, not automatic.
systems.agent_support: No native LLM/agent-loop primitive. Camunda is used to orchestrate AI/agentic steps via Connectors (there is a push toward 'agentic orchestration' with an AI-agent connector and Copilot for authoring in 8.8), and human-in-the-loop is a genuine first-class BPMN strength — User Tasks + Tasklist give durable human steps, and event-based gateways / message events model intervention points. But there is no built-in live agent-transcript streaming or mid-run inject-message/observability plane comparable to Aion's NOI; observability is Operate (token/incident inspection) and Tasklist (human tasks), which are process-level, not agent-transcript-level.
top_pain_points: BPMN .bpmn XML mixes process logic with diagram layout, so git diffs are enormous and noisy (a one-attribute change = hundreds of churned lines), making PR review and merge-conflict resolution painful; teams build custom diff filters to cope.
Merge conflicts on BPMN files in feature-branch workflows frequently can't be auto-merged — developers must manually re-apply their changes onto the updated model.

FEEL has sharp edges for developers: 1-based list indexing, NO type coercion (`"1" + 1` = null), null-on-missing-variable with no exception, and the engine/Operate 'swallow FEEL evaluation warnings' so a silently-null expression is hard to debug (open issue).
Logic hides off-canvas: real conditions/data-mappings live in FEEL fields inside property panels, so the diagram no longer tells the whole story — the visual-first promise erodes as complexity grows.

Operational heaviness of Camunda 8: running Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist + Optimize (esp. self-managed on Kubernetes) demands real DevOps effort; reviewers call it 'heavy' and still 'developer-centric'.
Process-definition versioning + in-flight instance migration is a manual, error-prone discipline when a model's shape changes.

The 'business analysts author, developers implement' pitch is aspirational — non-trivial models still need developers for FEEL, workers, connectors; analysts rarely own the executable model end-to-end.
notable_innovations: BPMN 2.0 is a portable, standardized, vendor-neutral visual language — a model can move between conformant engines; the notation itself is a decades-refined vocabulary for control flow that non-developers can read.
Diagram-first authoring where the picture is genuinely the shared artifact between business and engineering — the aspiration Aion's visual editor targets.
`@VariablesAsType` typed POJO (de)serialization on the worker gives near-serde ergonomics on the code side (reflection/Jackson, minimal boilerplate) — the boundary the Gleam side lacks.
Connectors + element templates: many tasks are configured in the model with zero worker code, cutting the per-activity handler tax.
User Tasks + Tasklist: first-class, durable human-in-the-loop steps — a strong pattern for Aion's intervention plane to learn from.

Web Modeler visual version-diff + changelists: acknowledges git-diffing a declarative artifact is the wrong altitude and provides a semantic diff instead — directly relevant to how Aion should diff visual workflows.
FEEL Copilot (8.8) and AI-agent connectors show the vendor direction toward AI-assisted authoring.

findings.cluster: Python data-orchestration engines with decorator-based economic authoring: Prefect (imperative @flow/@task), Dagster (declarative software-defined assets), Airflow (TaskFlow API decorators over a scheduler). All three reached "your function IS the task" by leaning on Python decorators + type hints, but they differ radically in HOW data crosses the task boundary (Prefect: result store w/ cloudpickle default; Airflow: XCom, now JSON-only; Dagster: IO managers, default = pickle to filesystem). The core lesson for Aion: Python gets automatic serialization essentially for free (pickle/cloudpickle is structural, no per-type codec), which is exactly the tax Gleam imposes by hand. The failure modes to avoid: pickle's opacity/non-portability/security, and hidden serialization surprises at the boundary.

systems.name: Prefect (v3)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Automatic and structural — NO per-type codec. Return values are Python objects passed in-memory between tasks in the same flow run; nothing is serialized on the happy path unless result persistence is enabled. When persistence IS on (needed for caching/retries/cross-process), the default serializer is pickle via cloudpickle, which serializes essentially any Python object by reflection with zero author effort. Alternative built-in serializers: JSONSerializer (Prefect-aware encoder that handles Pydantic BaseModels cleanly), compressed variants. Parameters (flow inputs) are validated/coerced via Pydantic using the function's type hints, and serialized to JSON for the API (512KB param cap). Source of truth = the Python type hints + Pydantic. Boilerplate per type: effectively zero for pickle; for JSON of custom types you may register a serializer, but Pydantic models work out of the box. This is the exact opposite of Gleam's hand-written codec-per-type tax.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Prefect does NOT use deterministic event-sourced replay (unlike Temporal/Aion). Durability = result persistence + task-level caching + retries. The author does NOT have to write deterministic code or think about replay/history — a retried task just re-runs the function; a cached task loads a persisted result keyed by a cache key (default derived from inputs). This is far less mental overhead than replay-based engines, but also weaker guarantees: mid-flow crash generally re-runs from the flow start unless results were persisted and cache keys hit. Versioning is lightweight (deployments carry versions) with none of the replay-compatibility pain of history-based engines, because there is no history to stay compatible with.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Among the lowest ceremony of any orchestrator. A minimal flow is ~6 lines and every line is intent — the decorator is the only glue. No entrypoint unwrap, no codec, no registration, no DAG object. Compared to Aion's Gleam ~27-line entrypoint + ~20-line codec per type, Prefect's equivalent is the bare function plus one decorator. The 'tax' only appears when you turn on persistence and hit a non-picklable object (DB connections, Pydantic-core internals) — then you must choose/write a serializer. But that is an exception path, not the default per-type cost.
systems.visual_editor: Rich web UI (Prefect Cloud + open-source UI) but code-first and READ-ONLY for topology. It visualizes flow runs as a state timeline / run graph with live state indicators (failed states highlighted), task-run radar, logs, and artifacts. Prefect recently added an Assets / @materialize decorator and an asset catalog with lineage graph (a deliberate move toward Dagster's model), showing 'reality not intention'. There is NO visual DAG editor / round-tripping — you cannot build or edit a workflow on a canvas; the UI is for operating and observing, not authoring.
systems.agent_support: Strongest agent story of the three via the Pydantic AI integration: Prefect is positioned as the durable-execution layer for Pydantic AI agents ('AI agents that resume from failure'), adding retries, observability, and durability around agent steps with minimal code. Tasks map naturally onto agent tool-calls/steps. It lacks a purpose-built live-transcript intervention plane like Aion's NOI, but the imperative model + artifacts + live run UI are a decent substrate for agent observability; human-in-the-loop is possible via pause/resume and input.
top_pain_points: Pickle-by-default surprises: many real objects don't pickle (DB connections like PIServer, some Pydantic V2 BaseModels trigger 'MockValSer cannot be converted to SchemaSerializer'); the fix is switching to JSON serializer, but that's a debugging trap discovered at runtime (GitHub #16557, #7624).
Memory buildup with result persistence: serializer objects for each result linger in memory even after being persisted to storage, hurting large-data tasks (#7624).
Persisted results are pickle-encoded and thus NOT language-agnostic / not readable via REST API — hard to expose results to non-Python HTTP clients (#17914/#17951).
Custom result serializers' deserialize() is sometimes not invoked when loading results, contradicting expectations (#4636).
