Dynamic DAG (built by running the flow) means the full graph isn't known until execution — weaker static analysis / harder to preview topology than a declared DAG.
Prefect 2->3 churn and the concurrency model (futures/.submit/.result, task runners) is a source of confusion vs the 'it's just a function' pitch.
notable_innovations: 'A workflow is just a decorated Python function' — the lowest-ceremony authoring model; dependencies inferred from ordinary data flow, no DAG object.
Structural/reflection serialization (cloudpickle) means ZERO per-type codec authoring on the default path — the direct antidote to Gleam's codec tax.
Pydantic-driven input validation/coercion straight from type hints — types are the schema, no hand-written decoder.
Explicit opt-in concurrency (.submit/.map + futures) keeps the simple case simple while allowing fan-out.
Pydantic AI durable-execution integration — a clean 'wrap your agent in durability' story.

systems.name: Dagster
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Mediated by IO MANAGERS — this is Dagster's signature (and most divisive) abstraction. When an asset returns a value, the IO manager HANDLES the write; when a downstream asset takes it as a parameter, the IO manager handles the read. The DEFAULT is FilesystemIOManager, which pickles the return value to storage/<asset_key> on local disk and depickles it downstream — so the default path is again automatic + structural (zero per-type codec, like Prefect). Typed/pluggable IO managers move data to real stores (DuckDBPandasIOManager, S3, Snowflake, Redshift) and can serialize e.g. a pandas DataFrame to parquet/a table; you write or configure the IO manager ONCE and every asset of that type is (de)serialized for free — serialization is factored out of the workflow code entirely. Source of truth = Python return type + the IO manager; type annotations on assets are used (and Config params require Pydantic-based dg.Config). Tradeoff: the boundary logic lives in a separate IO-manager object, which is powerful but an extra concept to learn.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Not replay/event-sourced. Durability = materialized assets persisted via IO managers + a materialization event log; recovery = re-materialize an asset (and Dagster knows staleness/lineage). The author does NOT reason about determinism or replay at all — you think about assets and their freshness. Re-execution can start from any asset because upstream values are persisted by the IO manager. Versioning is modeled first-class (code versions on assets + data versions) so Dagster can tell what is stale — arguably the best staleness/versioning story of the three, but it's about data freshness, not history-replay compatibility.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: The asset function itself is minimal (decorator + typed function) and the param-name-as-dependency trick removes explicit wiring. BUT Dagster front-loads ceremony: Definitions objects, resource/IO-manager configuration, dg.Config Pydantic classes for parameters, and project scaffolding. So per-asset it's low, but the SURROUNDING setup is the heaviest of the three. Real complaint: 'as problems get complicated you spend time on Dagster internals rather than the business problem.' Net: low glue in the asset body, high conceptual overhead in the framework scaffolding.
systems.visual_editor: The richest lineage UI of the three, but still code-first and NOT a visual editor. The Dagster UI (dagster-webserver / Dagit) shows a Global Asset Lineage graph you can drill into (logs, input/output metadata, run history) and a Materialize button to execute assets/backfills directly from the graph. It's the best 'see your whole data graph + operate it' surface. No canvas authoring / round-tripping — you cannot draw or edit the DAG; and the lineage view has known scaling pain (UI hangs rendering ~200+ assets, issue #20634).
systems.agent_support: Weakest explicit agent story of the three — Dagster is data-asset-centric, not agent-centric, and sensors are POLL-based rather than pub/sub, making event-driven / reactive agent loops awkward (a cited pain point vs Temporal). No live-transcript/intervention plane. That said, the asset+lineage+staleness model could map onto agent artifacts, and the @asset-as-outcome framing is philosophically close to 'what did the agent produce'.
top_pain_points: Steep learning curve + highly opinionated: developers 'spend time on Dagster internals rather than solving the actual problem'; IO managers, resources, Definitions, and Config are a lot of concepts before first value.

Cryptic type errors: generic annotations like Dict[str,str] without full params cause obscure runtime failures; stack traces bury the root cause under abstraction layers so the real reason/line is hard to find.

Default pickle IO manager breaks on non-picklable returns ('Error when pickling with fs_io_manager', discussion #16237) — same pickle trap as Prefect, and switching serialization formats is a long-standing feature request (#6902, #13592).
LLM/AI-assist unreliability: few in-depth examples in training data + frequent breaking changes between minor releases, so codegen assistants produce outdated/wrong Dagster code.
Job/graph composition (higher-order jobs from existing pipelines) is unwieldy and leans on sensors + conditional logic; event-driven work is hard because sensors poll.
UI lineage graph doesn't scale — hangs at a few hundred assets; docs frequently lag the current API.
notable_innovations: Software-defined assets: author the OUTCOME (the data), not the step — a genuinely different, higher-level authoring altitude; dependencies via parameter names read a static, analyzable graph.

IO managers factor ALL boundary (de)serialization out of workflow code into one reusable, swappable object — the same DataFrame code writes to local pickle in dev and Snowflake in prod with zero code change. (Strong idea for Aion: a pluggable 'boundary manager' separating codec from logic — maps to Aion's pluggable haematite storage direction.)
First-class code-version + data-version staleness model → the graph knows what's out of date and why.
Best whole-graph observability/operate surface (global lineage + one-click materialize/backfill).

systems.name: Airflow (TaskFlow API, v2.x/3.x)
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Data passes via XCom automatically: a task's return value is stored as an XCom and injected as the next task's argument with NO manual xcom_push/pull. Crucially, XCom is now JSON-ONLY by default — pickling was deprecated then REMOVED (enable_xcom_pickling gone; PR #43905/#44166). So the automatically-serializable set is limited to JSON-compatible types (dict/list/number/str/bool/None). For richer types you either (a) decorate the class with @dataclass or @attr.define (Airflow serializes these automatically), (b) implement serialize()/deserialize(data, version) methods on your class (versioned), or (c) configure a custom XCom backend (e.g. object storage) to offload large/blob data. So: automatic for JSON-shaped data; explicit-but-light for custom types; source of truth = Python types + optional serialize hooks. Less magic than Prefect/Dagster's pickle default, but safer (no pickle) — and it introduces a real per-nontrivial-type cost closer to Aion's codec tax, though much smaller.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Not replay-based. Durability = the metadata DB records every task instance's state; a failed task is retried or cleared+re-run, and XCom values persist task outputs in the DB. The author does not think about determinism/replay. Recovery is task-instance-level (re-run this task), not history-replay. Versioning historically weak (DAG code changes vs existing runs are a known footgun); Airflow 3 improves this. Scheduling/backfill (catchup) is a first-class concept the author DOES have to reason about.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: TaskFlow massively cut Airflow's legacy ceremony: no Operator instantiation, no xcom_pull, no explicit >> wiring — a task is a decorated function, roughly on par with Prefect for the body. BUT the DAG-level ceremony remains (@dag config: schedule, start_date, catchup, tags) and Airflow's environmental model (dags_folder, scheduler parsing) leaks into authoring. The biggest hidden 'boilerplate' is discipline, not lines: you must keep expensive work OUT of top-level/module code because the scheduler re-parses every DAG file (~every 30s) — a non-obvious constraint that shapes how you must write files.
systems.visual_editor: Mature web UI (Grid view, Graph view, Gantt, logs), and Airflow 3 modernized it — but it is observe/operate, code-first, and NOT a visual authoring canvas. You cannot build or edit a DAG by drawing; the Graph view renders the statically-declared DAG read-only and lets you trigger/clear/mark tasks. No code<->diagram round-tripping. (Block-based visual authoring is where competitors like Mage differentiate; Airflow deliberately stays code-first.)
systems.agent_support: Weakest / least agent-oriented — Airflow is a batch scheduler for data pipelines. No streaming/live-transcript, no human-in-the-loop intervention primitives beyond manual task clear/mark and sensors. Airflow 3 adds event-driven workflows and task isolation, narrowing the gap, but there is no first-class LLM/agent authoring or observability surface.
top_pain_points: Top-level code trap: the scheduler re-parses every DAG file on a short interval (~30s), so any import-time work (API calls, Variable lookups that hit the DB, list generation for dynamic mapping) runs constantly and tanks parse time — a famous, easy-to-hit footgun.
Dynamic task mapping scaling: large fan-outs (e.g. 100k mapped tasks) explode DAG-processing time to timeouts and trigger repeated slow (8s+) scheduler queries (discussion #36454).

Dynamic mapping is rigid: mapped tasks run all inputs in parallel with no built-in sequential mode; trigger_rule=ALWAYS is disallowed on mapped tasks; .expand_kwargs can't take some runtime-computed args / dynamic outlets (must be hardcoded via .partial).

XCom JSON-only limitation: rich/custom objects don't pass without @dataclass/@attr or hand-written serialize()/deserialize(version) — and XCom is a poor channel for large data (must stand up a custom backend + scale infra).
The DAG is a static, scheduler-bound artifact: truly dynamic run-time-shaped graphs are awkward, and DAG code changes vs in-flight runs cause versioning confusion.
Heavy operational surface (scheduler, workers, metadata DB, parsing) means authoring choices are constrained by deployment realities — high infra overhead vs 'just a function'.
notable_innovations: TaskFlow proved decorators can retrofit 'your function IS the task' onto even a legacy scheduler — dependencies inferred from passing return values, killing xcom_pull/>> boilerplate.
Automatic XCom passing: return value in, argument out, zero manual plumbing.
multiple_outputs=True auto-splits a returned dict into separately-addressable XCom keys — a neat ergonomic for fan-out of named outputs.

Safety-first serialization: JSON-only by default (dropped pickle) with @dataclass/@attr auto-serialization and explicit versioned serialize/deserialize hooks — a cleaner, more portable, more secure boundary than pickle, and the versioned-serializer pattern is worth stealing.
Custom XCom backends cleanly separate 'reference in metadata DB' from 'blob in object storage' — a pattern for keeping large payloads out of the control plane.

findings.cluster: Visual-first / low-code hybrid workflow systems (n8n, Windmill, Node-RED) — the visual-editor UX cluster. Common thread: composition happens on a canvas of nodes/steps wired by connections, data flows along the wires, and "drop to code" is the escape hatch. The three occupy distinct points on the visual↔code spectrum: Node-RED is visual-first with JS function nodes (messages on wires), n8n is visual-first with a strong node catalog + a per-item data model + JS/Python escape hatch (aimed at automation/AI, huge community), and Windmill is the code-FIRST hybrid — real scripts in any language are the atoms, the visual DAG (OpenFlow, a YAML spec) merely orchestrates them, and it round-trips cleanly between the canvas and files on disk. The dominant, universal pain across all three is version control / git diffing / merge of a visual graph: the moment a workflow is a JSON/graph blob, git review, blame, and merge break down. Windmill is the standout because it made the flow a set of plain files (flow.yaml + per-step script files + separated lockfiles) specifically to make PRs reviewable — the most directly relevant prior art for Aion, whose workflows are already code (Gleam) and whose challenge is the reverse: going FROM good textual authoring TO a good visual layer without losing the clean-diff property Windmill fought to get.

systems.name: n8n
systems.authoring_model: Visual-first node canvas. You author by dragging nodes (400+ integrations + core nodes: HTTP Request, Set, IF, Switch, Merge, Loop Over Items, Code) onto a canvas and drawing connections between output and input ports. Each node is configured via a side panel of fields; fields can hold static values or {{ expressions }}. The escape hatch is the Code node, which runs JavaScript or Python. A 'minimal workflow' isn't text you write — it's a Trigger node -> one or more action nodes wired left-to-right; the source of truth is a workflow JSON the editor generates (nodes[] with id/type/position/parameters + connections{}). Expressions use tokens like {{ $json.field }} (current item), {{ $('Node Name').item.json.x }} (reference another node), {{ $now }}, etc. There is no hand-written entrypoint; the engine drives nodes. AI/agent flows are built the same way with an AI Agent (LangChain) root node plus sub-nodes (model, memory, tools) plugged into it.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: This is n8n's defining data model and its biggest conceptual tax: data between nodes is ALWAYS 'an array of items', each item shaped { json: {...}, binary?: {...} }. Every node auto-maps over the items array (runs once per item). There is no schema, no types, no codecs — it's untyped JSON passed on wires, so 'serialization' is invisible/automatic, but the price is that authors must constantly reason about the array-of-items wrapper. The Code node has two modes ('Run Once for All Items' where you get $input.all() and must 'return an array of objects', vs 'Run Once for Each Item' where $json is the current item and n8n re-wraps your return). Choosing wrong yields the infamous 'Please return an array of objects, one for each item you would like to output' error. Item linking (which output item traces to which input item) is a second subtle layer authors trip over. Net: zero codec boilerplate, but a leaky, stringly-typed data model you must hold in your head.
systems.determinism_and_durability: n8n is an automation/orchestration engine, not a durable-execution/replay engine in the Temporal sense. It persists execution records (inputs/outputs per node) so you can inspect and 're-run', and queue mode (Bull/Redis) gives horizontal scale, but there is no deterministic replay-from-history programming model, no versioned event-sourced workflow, and no native global retry/exponential-backoff policy across a flow (retries are per-node; backoff must be hand-built by chaining a check-fail -> wait -> re-invoke-sub-workflow). Authors think about durability almost not at all at author time — which is the flip side: crash-exactly-resume semantics are not part of the model. Versioning of the workflow itself is a known weak spot (see pain points).
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Textually near-zero — there's no entrypoint, no codec, no type declarations; that's the whole low-code value prop. But 'ceremony' reappears as GRAPH ceremony: real logic is diluted across many small nodes (Set nodes to reshape data, Split Out / Loop Over Items nodes to handle arrays, IF/Switch nodes for branching), so a workflow that is ~15 lines of imperative code becomes a 40-node canvas. Community consistently reports you 'hit the need to start coding very fast' once logic is non-trivial, so real workflows end up as a mix of visual glue nodes wrapping Code nodes — the glue moves from text into node-count and wiring.
systems.visual_editor: Visual-FIRST and the core product. Polished canvas: drag/drop, live per-node execution with input/output data panels, 'pin data' to freeze a node's output while iterating, an expression editor with autocomplete over upstream node outputs, and NDV (node detail view) that shows the actual data flowing. Code<->diagram is one-directional: the diagram IS the source; the exported JSON is a serialization artifact, not something you hand-author. There is no round-trip 'edit the code, see the diagram update' story like Windmill — you live in the canvas. Debugging/observability in-editor is strong (see the data at each node, re-run from a node).
systems.agent_support: Strong and a major 2024-2026 focus. First-class AI Agent node (LangChain-based) as a 'root node' with pluggable sub-nodes: chat model, memory, and tools (any n8n node or sub-workflow can be a tool). Supports multi-agent orchestration (agents passing outputs to agents, branching on agent output). Human-in-the-loop is done with approval/wait nodes (e.g. 'Send and Wait for Response'). Observability of agent runs = the standard execution-log/data-panel view (inspect each step's in/out, retry a step). No dedicated live-streaming transcript intervention plane like Aion's NOI — it's the generic node-execution viewer applied to agent steps.
top_pain_points: No built-in workflow version control historically (open feature request #26707); git integration (source-control feature) exists but is Enterprise-gated and workflows are still stored as JSON.

Git diffs are near-useless: exported workflow JSON is dominated by node positions, UUIDs, and timestamps — '500 lines of JSON diff where 480 are position shifts and internal IDs'. Spawned a cottage industry of third-party visual-diff tools, normalizers, and 'n8n-as-code'.

Merge conflicts are unmanaged: n8n can't detect conflicts on workflows; two people editing the same flow => manual JSON conflict resolution. Recommended workaround is one-directional git flow (avoid concurrent edits entirely).
The array-of-items data model is a persistent conceptual gotcha: $input.all() vs $json, For-Each vs For-All Code-node modes, and the 'return an array of objects' error; item-linking is subtle.
Per-node execution overhead makes large workflows (70+ nodes) slow/impractical on Cloud; complex logic balloons the graph and users 'hit the need to code very fast'.
No native global retry / exponential backoff across a flow; durability guarantees are weak vs code-first durable engines (users migrate to Windmill/Temporal for strong guarantees).
notable_innovations: 'Pin data' — freeze a node's real output so you can iterate downstream nodes without re-hitting APIs; excellent authoring-loop ergonomic Aion could mirror for activities.
Live per-node input/output data panels in the editor: you always see the actual data shape flowing on each wire — makes an untyped system tolerable.
Expression editor with autocomplete over upstream node outputs and rich helper variables ($now, $json, $('Node').item).
Huge community + template library (thousands of shareable workflow JSONs) — network-effect authoring.
AI Agent node ecosystem where any node/sub-workflow becomes an agent tool — low-friction agentic composition.

systems.name: Windmill
systems.authoring_model:
Code-FIRST hybrid — the key case for Aion. The atom is a real script in Python/TypeScript/Go/Bash/SQL/PHP/Rust/etc. Each script's MAIN FUNCTION SIGNATURE is the source of truth: Windmill parses the typed params of `def main(x: int, y: str)` (or TS types) and auto-generates a JSON-schema-backed input form + an API endpoint + a triggerable job — 'no boilerplate in between'. A FLOW is then a DAG orchestrating these scripts, defined by the OpenFlow spec (a YAML document). A minimal flow.yaml is literally: `summary: ''
value:
  modules: []
schema: {type: object, properties: {}}`. On disk a flow is a `<name>.flow/` folder containing flow.yaml plus, per inline step, three files: the code (.py/.ts), metadata (.script.yaml), and a lockfile (.lock). You can author entirely in the visual flow editor OR entirely in files (VSCode + wmill CLI) and they round-trip. You can even edit the flow's YAML directly inside the visual editor.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Best-in-class of this cluster and directly instructive for Aion. Source of truth = the language's own types in the script's main() signature. Windmill introspects them to derive the JSON Schema (for the auto-form and for validation) — the developer writes NO codec; serialization across the step boundary is inferred from types via language reflection/AST parsing. Data between steps is passed by INPUT TRANSFORMS: each step field is either a static value or a JS expression evaluated against `flow_input` (the flow's params) and `results.<stepId>` (any prior step's output). E.g. step B reads `results.step_a.name`. So the boundary is: typed-params-in (auto-schema), plain-JSON-out, wired by small JS expressions. This is exactly the property Aion wants — 'author writes their types, serialization is derived' — achieved here by having the runtime introspect real language types rather than requiring hand-written Gleam codecs.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durable-ish execution: Rust engine on Postgres (state/queue) benchmarked ~13x Airflow; flows are restartable (resume from a failed/suspended step), support approval/suspend steps that cost nothing while waiting, retries, and error handlers per step. It is not a full event-sourced deterministic-replay model like Temporal — you don't write replay-safe code with a determinism constraint; steps are ordinary scripts and the engine persists step boundaries/results. Authors barely think about determinism (scripts are just scripts); durability is a runtime property of step boundaries, not an author obligation. Versioning: each deploy is versioned; git-sync commits scripts/flows/apps on every deploy so history lives in git.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Low intent-to-ceremony ratio for the CODE, higher for the YAML. The script itself is pure logic — write main() with typed args, done; the form, endpoint, and schema are generated. The ceremony is the OpenFlow YAML: authors report flows are 'more difficult to edit at the beginning' because you must learn OpenFlow (modules, input_transforms, branches, iterator). Community criticism: 'YAML is not real code' and you can't (yet) define a whole flow in pure Python — dynamic/programmatic flow generation means hand-emitting YAML. Windmill's answer was to keep each step a real code file and make YAML thin; they also promised decorator-based flow definition. Net: per-step boilerplate ~0; per-flow you pay an OpenFlow-YAML learning tax.
systems.visual_editor: Genuine code<->visual round-tripping — the differentiator. Drag/drop DAG flow editor with branches (conditional / branch-all-parallel), for-loops, retries, approval steps, error handlers, and a 'quick-connect' plug icon to visually pick `results.x`/`flow_input` sources instead of typing the JS. Crucially it round-trips with disk: `wmill dev` runs a local live-reload server where edits ON DISK reload the canvas and edits IN the canvas write back to flow.yaml/inline scripts. The inline script editor has a diff toggle (changes vs deployed). Live observability: every run logged with inputs/outputs/duration/status, real-time log streaming. So it's simultaneously code-first (files are real) and visual-first (canvas is real) — neither is a lossy projection of the other.
systems.agent_support: Strong and recent (2025): first-class 'AI Agent steps' inside flows — an agent step composes with branches/approval/error-handling like any other step, and can call Windmill scripts as tools (plus Windmill MCP exposes scripts/flows as tools to external agents). Providers: OpenAI/Anthropic/Azure/Mistral/Gemini/Groq/OpenRouter/etc. Flows containing agents can run in CHAT MODE (the flow UI becomes a conversational interface). Full observability on every tool call + LLM request, with RBAC/audit/versioning. Human-in-the-loop via native approval/suspend steps. This is close to Aion's ambition: agents as durable, observable, intervenable workflow steps — but without Aion's dedicated live-transcript NOI intervention plane.
top_pain_points: OpenFlow YAML learning curve: flows 'more difficult to edit at the beginning'; you must learn modules/input_transforms/iterator semantics — the visual editor hides some of this but power users editing YAML feel it.

'YAML is not real code' + no pure-Python/programmatic flow definition: users who want to DYNAMICALLY generate a whole workflow from config must hand-emit YAML; decorator-based flow authoring was requested and still maturing.

Lockfiles historically polluted the flow YAML and made git diffs noisy — Windmill had to split lockfiles into separate `!inline` files and split app scripts into their own files specifically to make PRs reviewable (tells you diffing a hybrid flow is genuinely hard).
Not ideal for complex/reconfigurable data pipelines per some users — positioned more as internal-tools/orchestration than a heavy data-pipeline DAG tool.

Open-source/licensing friction: vocal 'open letter' (#5014) accusing the per-worker/memory pricing of being a 'predatory' open-core model that discourages self-hosting; several enterprise flow features are gated.
Scope confusion / 'do one thing well': users unsure what Windmill IS (workflows? internal tools? UIs? cron?) — breadth dilutes the mental model.
notable_innovations: Types-as-schema: parse the script's main() signature to auto-derive input schema, form, and API — eliminates hand-written codecs by introspecting real language types (THE lesson for Aion's Gleam codec tax).

Files-as-source-of-truth flow: a flow is a folder of plain files (flow.yaml + per-step code + separated .lock), so git diff/PR/blame work on a visual workflow — solves the exact problem n8n/Node-RED can't.
`wmill dev` bidirectional live-reload: disk<->canvas round-trip so code-first and visual-first developers share one artifact.
Input-transform model: step inputs are small JS expressions over flow_input/results.<id>, with a visual 'plug' picker — a clean, inspectable data-passing layer between typed steps.
Separated lockfiles + per-file app scripts explicitly to make commits/PRs reviewable — a concrete engineering answer to 'how do you version-control a visual flow'.
AI agent steps that compose with normal flow control + chat-mode + MCP tool exposure — durable, observable agents-as-steps.

systems.name: Node-RED
systems.authoring_model: Flow-based visual programming, visual-first. You wire NODES (each a small function block from a palette) together with connections; MESSAGES flow along the wires. The canonical unit is the `msg` object (a JS object, conventionally with `msg.payload` as the body plus `msg.topic` etc.). The code escape hatch is the Function node: paste JavaScript that receives `msg`, mutates it, and MUST `return msg;` (returning a bare number/string errors; returning null halts the flow). Multiple outputs = return an array of msgs, one slot per output wire (e.g. `return [msg, null]` routes to output 1). The whole flow is stored as a single flows.json (array of node objects with id/type/x,y coords/wires[]). There's no textual authoring path you'd hand-write — you build on the canvas; flows.json is a serialization artifact. Reusable logic = Subflows (a subflow is your 'library' unit).
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Untyped and implicit — data is just the `msg` JS object flowing on wires; there is no schema, no types, no codec. Each node reads/writes `msg.payload` (and other msg.* props) by convention. This means zero serialization boilerplate but also zero contract: nothing enforces what shape a node emits vs the next expects, and there's no compile-time checking — mismatches surface only at runtime by inspecting messages. Message DESIGN (agreeing on payload shape across a team) is explicitly a documented discipline problem, not a language feature. Compared to Windmill's typed main() or Aion's typed Gleam, Node-RED sits at the far untyped end: maximal flexibility, minimal guarantees.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Essentially none as a first-class concept. Node-RED is an event/message router (originally for IoT/edge wiring), not a durable-execution engine: there's no replay-from-history, no event sourcing, no crash-exactly-resume. Flow 'context' (state) can be kept in memory or persisted (file/Redis), but that's manual. If a deploy overwrites flows and something breaks, the previous version is simply lost unless you exported it. Authors don't think about determinism at all; durability/state is a bolt-on the operator wires up (e.g. Redis context, circuit breakers) for production hardening. Subflow instances are independent and don't share context — a durability/resource footgun at scale (each subflow instance opens its own connections).
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Per-node ceremony is tiny (drop a node, set a couple fields) but the Function-node contract adds friction: every function must marshal through `msg`/`msg.payload` and remember `return msg;`. Real complexity manifests as WIRING sprawl — non-trivial logic becomes a dense mesh of nodes and wires ('spaghetti'), and common logic gets copy-pasted across flows (multiple places to fix a bug). Function nodes also accumulate redundant variable/code definitions. So, like n8n, the boilerplate isn't text — it's graph/wiring volume and duplicated function-node snippets. Unit-testing internal flow components is documented as HARD, which is itself a sign the abstraction leaks under real logic.
systems.visual_editor: Visual-FIRST, mature, the original flow-based canvas. Drag nodes from a palette, wire ports, Deploy. In-editor debug via the Debug node (dumps msg to a sidebar) and node status badges. It's beloved for rapid IoT/glue prototyping. But code<->diagram is one-way (diagram is source; JSON is export) and there's no live data-shape preview per wire like n8n's data panels — you inspect via Debug nodes. Projects mode adds git integration inside the editor (commit/push/pull, view history) but with gaps: no rebase or force-push from the UI (must drop to CLI), and it doesn't track WHO edited a flow or WHEN.
systems.agent_support: Weak / not a focus. Node-RED predates the LLM-agent wave and targets IoT/home-automation/integration wiring. You can bolt on LLM calls via HTTP Request or community LLM nodes, but there's no first-class AI-agent node, no agent memory/tool abstraction, no chat mode, and no live agent-transcript/intervention plane. Human-in-the-loop is DIY (e.g. dashboard nodes + wait). For Aion's NOI ambitions Node-RED offers little to emulate on the agent axis — its value here is purely the flow-wiring UX heritage.
top_pain_points: Git diffs are unreviewable: flows.json stores Function-node JavaScript as escaped single-line strings and includes node x/y positions and ids — diffs are noisy and code-in-JSON is hard to read/review.
No change attribution: the editor has auth but doesn't record who edited a flow or when; hard to revert or assign responsibility in mission-critical systems.
Manual backup burden without Projects/git: developers export flows to JSON by hand with naming conventions to keep versions — easy to lose the prior version on deploy.
Projects-mode git is limited: no rebase/force-push from the UI (CLI only); merge/review of visual flows remains painful.
Scaling is hard: designed for single edge instances; going to many instances 'isn't for the faint-hearted', needs external tooling (FlowFuse/K8s/Redis) and heavy dev-ops.

Maintainability at scale: duplicated flow fragments across flows, subflow instances each hold independent context/connections (resource waste), and unit-testing internal flow components is hard — the low-code model strains under real complexity.
notable_innovations: The msg-on-wire mental model: dead-simple, universally teachable data-flow metaphor (payload flows left-to-right, nodes transform it) — extremely low barrier for non-experts; a UX benchmark for 'obvious' data flow.
Multiple-output routing via returned array slots (`return [a, null]`) — a clean visual mapping of code branches to output wires.
Subflows as the reuse/library primitive in a purely-visual world.
Debug node + node status badges: lightweight, always-available in-canvas observability.
Massive palette + community node ecosystem for IoT/hardware/protocol glue — breadth of pre-built integrations.

findings.cluster: LangGraph + Mastra + AI-agent workflow frameworks (durable-agent authoring, streaming/HITL, and live observability + intervention)

systems.name: LangGraph (+ Functional API)
systems.authoring_model:
Python/JS. Two authoring surfaces. (1) Graph API — the core abstraction is an explicit state machine: define a shared State (a TypedDict / Pydantic model), write node functions `def node(state) -> dict` that return partial-state updates that get merged, then wire them with `add_node`/`add_edge`/`add_conditional_edges` from START to END and `.compile()`. Minimal shape:
```python
class State(TypedDict):
    topic: str
    output: str
def my_node(state: State):
    return {"output": llm.invoke(state['topic']).content}
g = StateGraph(State)
g.add_node('my_node', my_node)
g.add_edge(START, 'my_node')
g.add_edge('my_node', END)
app = g.compile(checkpointer=...)
app.invoke({'topic': 'cats'})
```
(2) Functional API (2025) — `@entrypoint` marks the workflow function, `@task` marks a durable unit of work, and you orchestrate with ordinary `if`/`for`/function calls; replay restores completed `@task` results from the checkpointer. This is the escape hatch from graph ceremony for people who just want durable imperative code. Agents (e.g. `create_react_agent`) are prebuilt graphs.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Structural, reflection-based — NOT the concentrated hand-written-codec tax Aion/Gleam suffers. State is a TypedDict/Pydantic model and node returns are plain dicts; the graph merges by key. Crossing the durability boundary (checkpointer) uses JsonPlusSerializer: ormsgpack/orjson with an extended-JSON fallback that natively understands LangChain types, datetimes, enums, Pydantic models. Source of truth = Python types. BUT the 'free serde' has sharp edges: generic Pydantic v2 types deserialize back to plain dicts instead of the model (issue #6102); msgpack is not thread-safe and can corrupt checkpoints (#4168); there were RCE advisories for unsafe msgpack/json deserialization (GHSA-g48c-2wqr-h844, GHSA-wwqv-p2pp-99h5) now gated behind LANGGRAPH_STRICT_MSGPACK. So: near-zero per-type boilerplate in the happy path, but implicit serialization means schema-drift and custom-type failures surface at runtime, not authoring time.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Durability via a checkpointer (in-memory, SQLite, Postgres) that snapshots the full state at every super-step. The author mostly does NOT write explicit steps in the Graph API — persistence is at node boundaries automatically. In the Functional API the author must consciously put non-determinism/side-effects inside `@task` so results are journaled and not recomputed on replay. Critically, multiple critics (Diagrid, others) argue LangGraph checkpoints are 'not true durable execution': no supervisor/watchdog/heartbeat, no automatic failure detection, recovery is a manual `invoke(None, config)` with the right thread_id, and there is no built-in coordination to stop two processes resuming the same thread_id concurrently (duplicate execution). Versioning/schema-drift of suspended threads is a known crisis ('orphaned thread'/schema drift). So durability is snapshot-persistence, not distributed guaranteed-completion.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Graph API: moderate ceremony — the state TypedDict + add_node/add_edge/START/END scaffolding is real glue that grows super-linearly with branches; critics say all adaptability must be explicitly hand-wired and it tends to create unintended loops. A trivial 1-step task is noticeably more code than the logic. Functional API dramatically cuts this: `@entrypoint`/`@task` over ordinary control flow is close to 'just your order of operations' — this is the shape Aion should study. Serialization boilerplate is low (reflection), unlike Gleam.
systems.visual_editor: LangGraph Studio is the standout and the most NOI-relevant artifact in this whole cluster. It is a code-first IDE (bridge between your editor and the running graph) that renders the graph as a canvas, shows nodes traversed + intermediate state live, streams node execution, and integrates LangSmith for trace/observability. Killer features: TIME-TRAVEL (step back through execution history, inspect why the agent decided X at any checkpoint, replay from a prior state non-destructively), INTERRUPTS/BREAKPOINTS (pause before/after a node), and STATE EDITING (directly modify AgentState at an interrupt or before replay; edits persist into thread history for traceability). You inspect/edit STATE and can edit code with hot-reload, but you do NOT author the graph by drawing it — no visual graph construction, inspection-first. Works best with LangChain/LangGraph agents only.
systems.agent_support: First-class — this is an agent framework, not a general workflow engine. Native streaming (token/step/state stream modes), human-in-the-loop via `interrupt()` and breakpoints with resume, and Studio provides live agent observability + intervention (the exact NOI feature set: live transcript of node execution, pause, edit state, resume, time-travel). Production observability via LangSmith. This is the closest existing analog to Aion's NOI plane and the bar to beat.
top_pain_points: Steep learning curve / conceptual overhead: developers repeatedly call the Graph API unnecessarily complicated with confusing docs; 'LangGraph hater to reluctant convert' is a common arc.

'Not a true agentic framework' / static graph rigidity: all adaptability must be explicitly built into the graph; it only loops/branches in ways the static structure allows, and empirically tends to create unintended loops and redundant processing.
Checkpoints ≠ durable execution: no watchdog/heartbeat, manual recovery via invoke(None, thread_id), no coordination against concurrent double-resume of a thread_id (Diagrid critique).
Serialization sharp edges: generic Pydantic v2 types deserialize to dicts (#6102); msgpack not thread-safe → corrupt checkpoints (#4168); RCE advisories forced LANGGRAPH_STRICT_MSGPACK.
Schema drift on suspended threads: evolving your State type crashes/​orphans already-suspended threads ('orphaned thread crisis'); Postgres checkpoint write-amplification/bloat at scale.
Studio/@task rough edges: @task checkpointing doesn't work with the API-server runtime-injected checkpointer (#6559); Studio is LangChain-centric and weaker with non-LC frameworks.
notable_innovations: LangGraph Studio time-travel + interrupt + live state editing — best-in-class visual agent debugging/intervention; the direct benchmark for Aion NOI, but Aion can leapfrog by making it DURABLE + distributed (Studio's replay is local/dev-centric) and code-language-agnostic.
Functional API (@entrypoint/@task): add durability/HITL/streaming to ordinary imperative code with minimal ceremony — a strong answer to Aion's 'run ceremony' pain worth emulating.
State-reducer model: nodes return partial updates merged by typed reducers — expressive shared-state semantics without hand-written merge code.
Multiple stream modes (values/updates/messages/debug) as a first-class API for live observability.

systems.name: Mastra
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Schema-first via Standard Schema (Zod / Valibot / ArkType). This is the single most instructive contrast for Aion's codec tax: in Mastra the Zod schema IS the source of truth AND the validator AND the TypeScript type (via z.infer) AND the (de)serialization contract at every step boundary — you write the shape ONCE and get runtime validation + static types + serialization for free. There is no separate to_json/decoder per type (unlike Gleam). Boilerplate per type ≈ one Zod object literal. Data crosses step boundaries as JSON validated against these schemas; step outputSchema becomes next step's inputSchema. This 'one schema, three uses' ergonomic is exactly what Aion should aim to replicate (or auto-derive) to kill the Gleam three-times-described-shape problem.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Built-in suspend/resume: workflows can `.suspend()` with a `suspendPayload` and later `.resumeStream()`; run status is one of success/failed/suspended/tripwire/paused, so human-handoff branches are explicit and typed. Durability depends on a pluggable storage adapter (the persistence layer for suspended runs/memory). The author reasons about suspension points explicitly rather than fully transparent replay — closer to explicit checkpointing than Temporal-style transparent determinism. Determinism/replay is less emphasized than in Temporal/Restate; storage adapters and memory primitives are an active area with open issues, so cross-crash durability is real but younger/less battle-hardened.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Low ceremony for the authoring shape itself — a minimal workflow is a step literal + a chained builder + `.commit()`, and the Zod schemas double as types+validation+serialization, so very little is pure glue. The main 'tax' is writing an inputSchema and outputSchema on every step and workflow (intentional, and it buys type-safety + serialization), plus `.commit()` as a required terminator. Overall one of the most economical authoring surfaces in the cluster; intent-to-glue ratio is high.
systems.visual_editor: Mastra Studio / Playground — a LOCAL dev environment (runs with the dev server) that is genuinely strong DX: chat directly with agents, dynamically switch models, tweak temperature/top-p live, run workflows and watch each step, and an Observability tab that renders traces highlighting model calls, tool executions, and workflow steps (AI Tracing filters framework noise). It is inspection/experimentation-first, not a visual graph BUILDER (you still author in TS). Production observability is via OpenTelemetry export to Langfuse/Braintrust/Arize/LangSmith/SigNoz/Opik. So: excellent local playground, but the deep live-observability is dev-local or delegated to third-party platforms — not a durable, production, intervene-in-a-live-run console like Aion's NOI aims to be.
systems.agent_support: First-class agent framework: agents, tools, memory primitives, workflows-with-agents-as-steps, streaming (`.stream()`), and suspend/resume for human-in-the-loop approvals. AI-specific tracing (token usage, latency, prompts/completions, decision paths, tool calls, memory ops). Human-in-the-loop is via typed suspend/resume rather than a live intervention console. Strong TS/full-stack DX (Next.js-friendly).
top_pain_points: 0.x instability: every minor release may carry breaking API changes (per their own SemVer note); teams report churn.
Observability being actively re-architected: a large umbrella issue (#6773) acknowledges many overlapping tracing complaints across GitHub/Slack/Discord — signals current tracing is inconsistent.
Monorepo support gaps (#6852): doesn't parse TS files by default; needs a build step or transpilePackages, a real friction for TS monorepos.
Storage-adapter / memory-primitive maturity: deployment and storage adapters are flagged active-issue areas; durable persistence less battle-tested than Temporal/Restate.
Supply-chain security incident (June 2026) — ecosystem/trust hit.
Playground observability is local/dev-first; serious production observability is offloaded to third-party OTel backends rather than owned end-to-end.
notable_innovations: Standard-Schema (Zod) as the single source of truth for type + validation + serialization at every step boundary — the model Aion should chase to eliminate the Gleam codec tax.
Typed suspend/resume with explicit suspendPayload and a status union (suspended/paused/tripwire) making human-in-the-loop a first-class, type-checked control-flow state.
Local Playground DX: chat with agents, hot model-swap, live temperature/top-p tuning, per-step trace view — a fast inner loop worth emulating for authoring UX.
Fluent control-flow combinators (.then/.parallel/.branch/.foreach/.dowhile) that read like order-of-operations.

systems.name: Inngest AgentKit
systems.authoring_model: TypeScript, code-only. Compose agents into networks: `createAgent({ name, description, system, model, tools })`, `createTool({ name, description, parameters: z.object(...), handler })`, and `createNetwork({ agents, router, state })`. The `router` is a function `(state) => nextAgent` giving deterministic (or model-driven) routing between agents; agents are stateless with a goal + tools, and shared context lives in Network State. Minimal shape is an agent + a tool + a network with a router. Inference calls use Inngest's `step.ai` so each model call is a durable, retried, cached step. Runs on top of the Inngest durable-execution engine (step functions).
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Zod-schema-first for tool parameters (source of truth = Zod → TS types + validation), same 'write-once' ergonomic as Mastra. Network State is serialized as JSON in the durable step context; state crossing the durability boundary is plain JSON. Low per-type boilerplate: a Zod object per tool, no hand-written codecs. Inherits Inngest's step-function serialization (step outputs must be JSON-serializable).
systems.determinism_and_durability: Strong — this is AgentKit's differentiator over LangGraph/Mastra. It runs on Inngest's durable execution engine: `step.ai` and `step.run` make each inference/tool call a journaled step with automatic retries and result caching; workflows can pause indefinitely (`step.waitForEvent`) for hours/days awaiting user approvals, scheduled times, or webhooks without holding resources, then resume. This is genuine durable execution (supervised, retried, resumable) rather than snapshot checkpointing — closer to Temporal/Restate. Author reasons in terms of steps; determinism concerns are the standard 'wrap side-effects in step.run' discipline.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Low-to-moderate. The agent/tool/network literals are concise and Zod carries the type/serialization load. The durability discipline (put side-effects in step.run / step.ai) is real but light. More opinionated toward multi-agent networks, so a single trivial task carries some network/router framing overhead you don't strictly need.
systems.visual_editor: No visual graph authoring. Observability is via the Inngest Dev Server / dashboard (step-by-step run timeline, retries, event waits, replays) plus 'Insights'. The standout is useAgent — the real-time front-end streaming layer (see below) — which is the intervention/observability-adjacent surface. There is no time-travel-state-editing canvas like LangGraph Studio; strength is the durable run timeline + real-time UI streaming, not a live state-editor.
systems.agent_support: First-class durable multi-agent: agents, tools (incl. MCP), networks with deterministic routing, persisted Network State, indefinite waits for human approval, retries/cancellations. The novel piece for Aion's NOI comparison is useAgent — a React hook (`const { messages, sendMessage, status } = useAgent()`, wrapped in `<AgentProvider>`) that streams durable backend agent workflows to the frontend via 'parts-based streaming' (discrete text/tool/reasoning/data parts that update independently), with automatic reconnection that resumes exactly where execution stopped and no WebSocket/state-store plumbing for the developer. It claims to support retries/cancellations/human-approval from the UI but the mechanics of approval/interruption flowing back to the backend are undocumented — a real gap. This is the closest cluster analog to Aion's live-transcript-to-ops-console goal, and Aion's durable-NOI + explicit intervention (inject-message/cancel) could clearly leapfrog it.
top_pain_points: useAgent human-in-the-loop/interruption/approval mechanics are underdocumented — how frontend approvals flow back to the durable backend isn't explained; error-handling and reconnection internals opaque.
Requires buying into the Inngest platform/event model and its hosting/dev-server — heavier operational commitment than a library.
Opinionated multi-agent-network framing adds conceptual overhead for simple single-agent or plain-workflow use cases.
Younger ecosystem/docs depth vs Temporal; deterministic-router design means adaptive behavior must be encoded in routing logic.
State is JSON-in-step-context — no rich typed state channels/reducers like LangGraph; larger shared state must be managed manually.
notable_innovations: useAgent parts-based real-time streaming of DURABLE backend agent workflows to React with automatic resume-where-you-left-off and zero WebSocket/state plumbing — the strongest 'durable + real-time to the frontend' story in the cluster, and the most direct competitor to Aion NOI's live-transcript surface.
Genuine durable execution under agents (step.ai ret/cache + indefinite waitForEvent) rather than checkpoint snapshots — human approvals can pause for days without holding resources.
Deterministic router abstraction separating orchestration/routing from agent logic.
MCP-native tooling.

systems.name: Temporal (durable-agent positioning)
systems.authoring_model: Not an agent framework — a general durable-execution engine (workflows in code: Go/Java/Python/TS/.NET) positioning itself as 'the durability layer for the entire agent ecosystem.' You author normal workflow code; agent frameworks plug in on top. Flagship integration is the OpenAI Agents SDK: every agent invocation runs inside a Temporal Activity, the agent loop runs as a Workflow, and `activity_as_tool` auto-generates OpenAI-compatible tool schemas from an Activity function signature (wrapping the activity as an agent tool with durable tool-calls). Also integrates Pydantic AI, Vercel AI SDK, Amazon Bedrock Strands.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Language-native data converters (payload codecs) — reflection/serde-style per SDK (Python dataclasses/Pydantic, Go structs, etc.), source of truth = your language types. The `activity_as_tool` helper is notable: it DERIVES the LLM tool schema from the Activity's typed signature automatically, eliminating hand-written tool schemas — directly relevant to Aion's ambition to auto-derive boundaries from types. Boilerplate is low relative to Aion/Gleam because the SDK's converter handles (de)serialization; determinism constraints (no nondeterminism in workflow code) are the main author burden.
systems.determinism_and_durability: The gold standard for transparent durability: event-sourced history + deterministic replay; the author writes ordinary code and durability is transparent, but MUST obey determinism rules (all nondeterminism/side-effects go in Activities) and manage workflow versioning/patching for long-running or in-flight workflows (a well-known pain). For agents this means rate-limited LLMs auto-progress when capacity returns, network blips retry downstream calls, and a crash mid-task resumes exactly where it left off — saving tokens/compute. This is real distributed durable execution (supervised, horizontally scalable), which is the bar the LangGraph-checkpoint critics point to.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Moderate-to-heavy setup ceremony (workflow/activity split, worker registration, task queues, determinism discipline, versioning) — the classic Temporal tax — but low per-type serialization boilerplate. For agents specifically, the integration hides much of it: activity_as_tool + workflow-wrapped agent loop is fairly concise. Aion shares Temporal's workflow/worker-process split, so Temporal's ceremony critiques are the most transferable to Aion.
systems.visual_editor: Temporal Web UI shows workflow execution history, event timeline, and state — strong for durable-run inspection and replay/debugging, but it is NOT an agent-transcript live-intervention console and not a graph authoring canvas. No time-travel state-editing UX like LangGraph Studio; intervention is via signals/queries and the CLI/API, not a live ops-console for agent transcripts. This is precisely the gap Aion's NOI targets.
systems.agent_support: Retrofit rather than native: agent support comes through SDK integrations (OpenAI Agents SDK, Pydantic AI, Vercel AI SDK, Bedrock Strands) layering agent loops onto durable workflows. Human-in-the-loop via Signals/Updates (mature, durable) and Queries for state. No built-in live agent transcript streaming/observability plane — that's exactly where Aion's durable NOI could differentiate against Temporal's 'durability-only, bring-your-own-agent-UX' position.
top_pain_points: Determinism discipline: workflow code must be deterministic; accidental nondeterminism is a classic footgun and the #1 authoring gotcha carried into agent loops.
Workflow versioning/patching for long-running or in-flight workflows is painful — directly relevant since agents run long.
Heavy operational + conceptual overhead (workflow/activity split, workers, task queues) — high barrier vs a library.
No native agent observability/intervention UI — you get durable-run history, not live agent-transcript streaming or mid-run message injection.
Agent support is integration-shaped, so ergonomics depend on the third-party SDK; some duplication between agent-framework state and Temporal history.
notable_innovations: activity_as_tool: auto-derives LLM tool schemas from typed Activity signatures — a concrete 'types are the source of truth, no hand-written boundary schema' pattern Aion should emulate for its activity/tool boundary.
Transparent deterministic-replay durability as the reference model that snapshot-checkpoint frameworks are measured against.

Positioning as the neutral durability substrate under many agent SDKs — validates Aion's 'durable substrate' thesis, and highlights the open lane: durability + native live agent observability/intervention together.

systems.name: Restate (durable-agent positioning)
systems.authoring_model: Durable-execution engine (not an agent framework) positioned for 'durable agents' as serverless HTTP handlers. An agent = an HTTP handler containing the agent loop; LLM calls and tool executions are wrapped as durable steps recorded in a journal. Integrates with OpenAI SDK, Vercel AI SDK, and Pydantic AI so you keep your framework's authoring shape but get Restate durability underneath. Emphasis: agents should be 'serverless and durable' — no long-lived process required.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Language-native serde per SDK; step results (LLM responses, tool outputs) are journaled as serialized values. Source of truth = your language types via the framework you bring. Like Temporal, low per-type codec burden relative to Gleam; the durability wrapping is the added surface, not hand-written serialization.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Journal-based durable execution built from first principles: each step (LLM call, DB query, tool invocation) is recorded; on crash Restate detects failure, restarts the handler, replays completed steps from the journal (returning recorded results instantly, no re-execution or duplicate side-effects) and resumes from the first incomplete step. This is genuine replay-based durable execution with supervision — the same class the LangGraph-checkpoint critics contrast favorably. Author reasons in durable-step terms; framing is 'fault tolerance across frameworks without handcuffs.'
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Low-to-moderate: you wrap LLM/tool calls as durable steps but otherwise keep your agent framework's authoring shape. Lighter operational model than Temporal (serverless handlers, no always-on worker fleet). Per-type serialization is not the pain point; step-wrapping discipline is.
systems.visual_editor: Restate UI provides run/journal inspection and invocation state, useful for durable-run debugging, but (like Temporal) it is not a live agent-transcript intervention console or a graph-authoring canvas. No LangGraph-Studio-style time-travel state editing. Observability is durable-run/journal-centric.
systems.agent_support: Framework-agnostic durability layer for agents (OpenAI SDK, Vercel AI SDK, Pydantic AI). Human-in-the-loop via durable awakeables/promises (pause for external input/approval and resume). Like Temporal, no native live agent-observability/streaming plane — again the open lane Aion NOI targets.
top_pain_points: Still a durability substrate, not an agent authoring/observability product — you bring the agent framework and its UX; no native live agent transcript/intervention UI.
Step-wrapping discipline: developers must correctly mark LLM/tool calls as durable steps to get exactly-once side-effect semantics.
Serverless/handler model + journal is a newer paradigm with a smaller ecosystem than Temporal; operational mental model shift.
Journal growth / long-running agent runs need management (unbounded loops, large tool outputs).
Cross-framework integrations mean durability quality depends on how cleanly the framework's loop maps to journaled steps.
notable_innovations: 'Durable AI loops without handcuffs': add journal-replay durability under existing agent frameworks (OpenAI/Vercel/Pydantic) without adopting a new authoring model — a pragmatic wedge.
Serverless + durable agents (handlers, not long-lived processes) — a different durability topology than Temporal workers.
Awakeables/durable promises for clean human-in-the-loop pause/resume.

Confirms the market pattern: durability substrates (Restate/Temporal) plus agent frameworks (LangGraph/Mastra/AgentKit) — with native durable LIVE observability+intervention as the unclaimed intersection Aion can own.

systems.name: Protobuf / gRPC (schema-first codegen)
systems.authoring_model: Schema-FIRST. You write a `.proto` IDL file: `message Person { string name = 1; int32 age = 2; }` plus `service`/`rpc` declarations. `protoc` (or Buf) generates types + serializers + client/server stubs in every target language. The `.proto` is the single source of truth; the Gleam type, Rust struct, TS type are all GENERATED artifacts, never hand-written. Field numbers (tags) are the wire identity and must be stable forever.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Zero hand-written codecs — the whole point. Wire format is a compact tagged binary derived mechanically from field numbers. Cross-language for free: same .proto -> Go, Rust, TS, Python, (and there are community protobuf codegens; no first-class Gleam/BEAM target though — this is the gap for Aion). Source of truth = the schema (proto), NOT the language types. Cost: you leave your language's type system to author in a separate DSL.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Not a workflow engine, but the serialization model is highly relevant: proto wire format is well-specified and stable, and Buf enforces backward/forward compatibility via breaking-change detection (`buf breaking`). Field-number stability = durable-history-safe by construction: old bytes decode against new schema as long as tags are preserved and only-add/never-reuse discipline is followed. This is the property Aion needs for replaying old workflow history.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Near-zero per-type codec boilerplate — but you pay a DIFFERENT tax: a whole build pipeline (protoc/buf, plugins, generated-file management), a separate .proto language to learn, and the ceremony of tag numbers. Devs complain the traditional toolchain is 'dated and easy to use incorrectly', dependency management is ad-hoc (Makefiles/bash to fetch protos), and generated code causes merge conflicts if committed.
systems.visual_editor: No visual editor. Buf Studio provides a schema registry + web request-explorer, and there's a documentation-generation story, but authoring is text (.proto) only.
systems.agent_support: None natively. Schema-first is agent-neutral; it's just a data contract. Relevant only as a serialization substrate.
top_pain_points: Toolchain is dated / easy to misuse; no standardized dependency management (Buf improved this but it's not universal) — kmcd.dev 'Working with Protobuf in 2024'
Generated files shouldn't be committed (merge conflicts) but must be built at CI/build time — extra pipeline complexity
Node/JS ecosystem specifically: poor protobuf.js / grpc-node compatibility is 'a huge pain-point' (grpc-node issue #528)
Manual backward-compat vigilance without Buf's breaking-change linter; field-number reuse silently corrupts data
Authoring lives OUTSIDE your language's type system — you describe the same shape twice (proto + however you use it idiomatically)
No first-class BEAM/Gleam codegen target — would need a custom protoc plugin
notable_innovations: Field-number tags as permanent wire identity => trivially safe schema evolution / durable-history replay (exactly Aion's need for old-history decode)
Buf's `buf breaking` machine-checkable compatibility gate — turns 'don't break the wire' from discipline into CI enforcement
One schema -> N languages, so a polyglot system (Aion: Gleam workflow + Rust worker) shares ONE contract with zero drift

systems.name: Cap'n Proto (schema-first, zero-copy)
systems.authoring_model: Schema-FIRST like protobuf: a `.capnp` schema with numbered fields, but the wire format is bit-for-bit identical to the in-memory layout (C-struct fixed offsets), so 'parsing' is a pointer cast — zero-copy. Codegen produces accessor classes per language. Authored by Kenton Varda (ex-protobuf maintainer) to fix protobuf's parse overhead.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Zero hand-written codecs; generated accessors. Source of truth = the .capnp schema. The zero-copy trade means the generated API is builder/reader-shaped rather than plain structs — you get `.getName()`/`.initName()` accessors, not idiomatic language records. Rust developers call it 'extremely unergonomic to use' precisely because the API mirrors the wire layout instead of your types.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Wire = memory layout, so serialization is fully deterministic and stable. Schema evolution is STRICTER than protobuf: renames/type-changes require understanding bit-offset mapping; discipline around ordinals/IDs is mandatory and there's 'no validation that ensures schema changes are valid' (no diff/before-after tooling). Durable but with sharper edges than Buf-guarded protobuf.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Zero codec boilerplate, but the ergonomic tax is high: the generated API leaks the zero-copy machinery into everyday code. Not the model you'd want an Aion author writing business logic against.
systems.visual_editor: None.
systems.agent_support: None. Pure serialization substrate.
top_pain_points: 'Extremely unergonomic' generated API — builder/reader accessors instead of native structs (Swatinem, 'A Rustacean's view on gRPC and Cap'n Proto', 2024)
Stricter schema evolution than protobuf; renames/type changes need bit-offset understanding
No schema-diff / breaking-change tooling to validate that an evolution is safe
'Real zero-copy' is partly a fallacy in practice once you touch the data across boundaries (dev.to autopsy)
Smaller ecosystem; protobuf remains the 'safe default' as of 2026 (techbytes 2026 comparison)
notable_innovations: Zero-copy: wire format == memory layout, eliminating a parse pass entirely (matters only on the very hottest paths)
Same numbered-field durable-evolution discipline as protobuf

systems.name: tRPC (types-first, TypeScript inference)
systems.authoring_model: Types-FIRST, zero schema, zero codegen. You define server procedures in TS; the CLIENT type is INFERRED directly from `typeof appRouter` — 'import your backend function signatures into your frontend.' Runtime input validation is delegated to a validator (usually Zod) attached per-procedure: `t.procedure.input(z.object({...})).query(...)`. So static types come from inference; runtime wire-validation comes from the Zod schema you attach.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: For the STATIC contract: zero boilerplate — pure inference, 'define it once, use it everywhere', server field rename => client compile error. For the RUNTIME/wire boundary: you still hand-author a Zod schema per input (see Zod entry). JSON over HTTP by default; no binary format. Source of truth = TypeScript types (+ colocated Zod for runtime).
systems.determinism_and_durability: Not a durability system. The inference model is compile-time only and evaporates at runtime — there is NO standalone artifact you could persist/version as a durable contract. This is the key limitation vs Aion's needs: inference gives you no serializable schema to replay old history against; you'd need the emitted Zod schemas for that.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Lowest ceremony of any system for the static half — a procedure is basically its handler. But it's TypeScript-language-locked and monorepo-shaped: the client must import the server's TYPES, so it only works when both ends are TS in one repo. For a Gleam-workflow + Rust-worker split, tRPC's core mechanism (structural type inference across the wire) is not directly portable.
systems.visual_editor: No visual editor; the DX win is IDE autocomplete/inline errors from inferred types, not a diagram.
systems.agent_support: None native, but the ergonomics (a procedure ~= a typed function) are the DX bar Aion's 'kill the ceremony' goal is chasing.
top_pain_points: TypeScript-ONLY; useless for polyglot / public APIs — 'not designed for polyglot architectures' (Encore comparison, InfoQ)
Requires a monorepo (or published type packages); lose guarantees otherwise (tRPC FAQ)
TS language-server degrades badly as router/type size grows — 'type resolution becomes slower and slower (borderline unusable)' at scale (Medium, Julien Barbay)
No emitted schema artifact => no built-in versioning/documentation for external consumers; people move to oRPC/OpenAPI for that
Still need Zod (hand-authored) for actual runtime validation — inference alone doesn't validate untrusted input
notable_innovations: Structural type INFERENCE across a process boundary => the contract is literally your function signatures, zero declaration overhead (the north-star DX)
Compile-time breakage on contract drift with no codegen step at all
'Define once, use everywhere' — the exact author experience Aion wants (~just your types + order of operations)

systems.name: Encore.ts (types-first, compiler extracts schema)
systems.authoring_model: Types-FIRST but with a COMPILER that extracts a real schema — the hybrid that's most relevant to Aion. You write plain TS interfaces + generic annotations (`Query<number>`, `Header<'X-My-Header'>`, `Min<N>`, `IsEmail`) and declare an endpoint: `export const ep = api<Request, Response>({method:'POST', path:'/x'}, async (req)=>{...})`. At BUILD time Encore's static analyzer parses the TS types and generates a schema (JSON-schema / a Protobuf file) from them.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Author writes ZERO codecs AND zero separate schema — the natural TS type IS the contract. The compiler mechanically derives it. At runtime a Rust core reads the generated schema and PRE-COMPUTES a per-endpoint decoder/encoder, doing request validation in Rust before JS ever runs. Source of truth = the language types; the schema is a generated intermediate the author never touches. Claims 9x Express / 3x Hono because validation is precompiled in Rust, not per-request Zod.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Because a concrete schema is EMITTED (not just inferred-and-evaporated like tRPC), there IS a durable, versionable artifact — closer to what a durable engine needs. The precomputed decoder/encoder per endpoint is deterministic. (Encore is an API framework, not a workflow engine, so no replay story, but the 'types -> emitted schema -> precompiled codec' pipeline is the exact shape Aion could adopt for Gleam.)
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Essentially zero serialization ceremony: an endpoint is its handler plus a types annotation. This is the strongest proof that 'types as source of truth, tooling extracts the wire schema' can give BOTH tRPC-level authoring economy AND a real emitted contract — without derive macros in the language, by using a build-time analyzer + a native runtime.
systems.visual_editor: Encore has a local dev dashboard (API explorer, traces, architecture/service diagram auto-derived from code), i.e. code-first with generated visualization — not a visual authoring editor. Relevant precedent for Aion's ops-console.
systems.agent_support: None specific.
top_pain_points: Locks you into the Encore framework + its build toolchain and Rust runtime; not a drop-in library
Static analysis supports a defined subset of TS type constructs — exotic/dynamic types don't extract cleanly (docs enumerate supported types)
Validation-in-Rust is fast but opaque; custom serialization logic must fit the annotation model (Min/Max/IsEmail/etc.), not arbitrary code
Younger ecosystem; heavier all-in-one framework vs a focused codec lib
notable_innovations: Build-time STATIC ANALYSIS of language types -> emitted schema -> precompiled native codec: gets 'declare shape once, never write a codec' WITHOUT language derive macros — directly transplantable to Gleam (parse Gleam types, emit codecs)
Validation pushed below the language runtime (Rust) for speed + DoS resistance — mirrors Aion's Gleam-workflow/Rust-substrate split
Annotations-as-types (`Min<N>`, `IsEmail`) fold validation rules into the type itself, so one declaration = type + validator + wire contract

systems.name: Effect Schema / Zod (runtime schema that IS the type)
systems.authoring_model: Types-first via a RUNTIME schema value. You write ONE schema expression — `const Person = Schema.Struct({name: Schema.String, age: Schema.Number})` (Effect) or `z.object({name: z.string(), age: z.number()})` (Zod) — and derive the static type from it (`type Person = Schema.Schema.Type<typeof Person>` / `z.infer<typeof Person>`). The single declaration yields: the TS type, a runtime validator, and (Effect) a two-way decoder/encoder. Effect Schema uniquely models `Schema<Type, Encoded, Requirements>` — an explicit bidirectional transform between an in-memory type and its Encoded/wire form.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: One declaration = static type + validator + encoder + decoder, no drift possible. Effect's encoded-vs-type distinction is the cleanest formalization of 'the shape crossing the boundary' — it separates the domain type from the wire type and transforms between them. Zod 4 added built-in JSON-Schema export. Source of truth = the schema VALUE (which also produces the type), i.e. schema and type are the same declaration. This is achievable in a no-macro language: a codec is an ordinary value you compose — exactly what Gleam's `gleam-community/codec` and `convert_json` do.
systems.determinism_and_durability: The schema is a first-class runtime VALUE, so it can be persisted, versioned, and used to decode old data deterministically — better durability story than pure inference. Effect's explicit Encoded type means the wire contract is nameable and stable. Composable transforms let you write migrations as schema-to-schema functions.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Much less than hand-written codecs, but NOT zero: you still author a schema expression per type (a builder chain), roughly proportional to field count. It's ONE declaration instead of three (type + encoder + decoder), which is the core win. Effect Schema specifically is criticized as verbose and having a steep learning curve; Zod is lighter but v4 had a notable perf regression and single-use schema perf is poor.
systems.visual_editor: None; these are code libraries. Zod 4's JSON-Schema export enables downstream tooling (docs, form generators).
systems.agent_support: None native, though schema values are commonly used to define LLM tool/function-call argument shapes (validated), which is adjacent to Aion's agent NOI work.
top_pain_points: Effect Schema: verbose, functional-composition style unintuitive for many; steep learning curve (Medium/Effect community)
Zod v4 became ~17x slower than v3 in cases; single-use schemas (per-render) drop to ~6 ops/ms (dev.to, LogRocket 'Why is Zod slow?')
'Only as good as your discipline' — vague error messages and confusing schemas when treated as a magic box
You still write a schema per type (builder chain) — reduced, not eliminated, boilerplate
Runtime schema construction has non-trivial cost when schemas are built rather than reused
notable_innovations: ONE declaration yields type + validator + encoder + decoder — the canonical 'declare a shape once' answer, and it needs NO language macro (a schema is just a composable value)
Effect's `Schema<Type, Encoded, Requirements>`: explicit, nameable, two-way transform between domain type and WIRE type — the precise abstraction Aion's workflow<->activity boundary needs
Schema-to-schema transforms as first-class migrations (versioning as code, not tribal discipline)
Zod 4 built-in JSON-Schema export: runtime schema -> portable schema for polyglot/doc tooling

systems.name: Smithy / OpenAPI (schema-first IDL codegen)
systems.authoring_model: Schema-FIRST IDL. Smithy: a protocol-agnostic `.smithy` model of shapes + operations + services, decorated with TRAITS (`@http`, `@required`, `@range`) that are orthogonal to the protocol; pluggable code generators emit clients/servers/docs per language and per protocol (AWS JSON, restJson1, RPCv2 CBOR...). OpenAPI: a YAML/JSON contract for HTTP APIs, from which generators emit types + clients/servers. Both keep the contract OUTSIDE the language.
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Zero hand-written codecs — everything is generated from the model. Smithy's differentiator: PROTOCOL is decoupled from the MODEL via traits, so one model -> many wire protocols, swappable at runtime without rebuilding the client. Source of truth = the IDL model. Cost: a separate modeling language + generator toolchain, same 'author the shape outside your types' tax as protobuf.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Contract-first artifacts are inherently versionable/durable and are the documentation. Smithy emphasizes 'generated code is valid without manual editing, readable, and correct by default' and API-standards enforcement via traits — machine-checkable governance. Good durable-contract properties, but no workflow-replay semantics (it's an API-contract tool).
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Zero per-type codec boilerplate; you pay in IDL authoring + generator setup + build pipeline. Heavier upfront than a code-first library; justified when you need many languages/protocols/docs from one contract (AWS's exact use case).
systems.visual_editor: No first-class visual authoring. OpenAPI has a large tooling ecosystem (Swagger UI / editor, doc renderers, mock servers) — the richest 'schema -> generated UI/docs' story of any system here. Smithy has a CLI + trait-driven doc generation.
systems.agent_support: Smithy is being positioned for 'Agentic AI and safe tool use' — models as the source of truth for typed, validated LLM tool definitions (AWS blog). Directly relevant precedent for Aion's agent direction: a durable model that both runs code AND describes tools to an LLM.
top_pain_points: Separate IDL to learn + generator/build toolchain to operate; heavier than code-first
OpenAPI generators are notoriously inconsistent in quality across languages; generated clients often need post-processing
Contract lives outside the code, so it can drift from implementation unless enforced (the inverse of the types-first problem)
Smithy ecosystem is AWS-centric; fewer community language targets than protobuf/OpenAPI
Trait system is powerful but has real learning curve for authors
notable_innovations: Smithy: protocol/model DECOUPLING via traits — one model, many wire protocols, swappable at runtime (unmatched by protobuf)
Traits enable machine-enforced API standards / governance across an org
Model-as-source-of-truth reused for LLM tool schemas (agentic use) — one contract serves code, docs, AND agent tool definitions
OpenAPI's schema -> live docs / mock / form tooling is the benchmark for 'schema drives a visual/interactive surface'

systems.name: Rust serde (types-first via derive macro)
systems.authoring_model: Types-FIRST. You annotate your existing type: `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] struct Person { name: String, age: u32 }`. The `serde_derive` PROC MACRO reads the type's AST at compile time and generates Serialize/Deserialize impls. One derive => codec for every serde format (JSON, MessagePack, CBOR, bincode...). The type IS the source of truth; the codec is generated invisibly at compile time. This is the model Aion's RUST worker already enjoys — and precisely what Gleam CANNOT do (no derive/macros).
systems.serialization_ergonomics: The gold standard for authoring economy: ONE attribute, zero codec code, format-agnostic. Field-level attributes (`#[serde(rename)]`, `skip`, `default`, `flatten`) tune the wire mapping inline. Source of truth = the Rust type. Critically: serde 'boils it all away' to plain generated source — NO runtime reflection, so it's as fast as hand-written. This asymmetry (Rust worker gets codecs free, Gleam workflow does not) is the exact origin of Aion's pain concentration.
systems.determinism_and_durability: Generated codec is deterministic and monomorphized. Wire compatibility is the author's responsibility (rename/version via attributes); no built-in schema artifact or breaking-change checker (unlike Buf) — you evolve types carefully. Adequate for durable history if you're disciplined with field attributes and defaults.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: As close to zero as it gets — a derive line. The lesson for Aion: the ENTIRE codec tax on the Rust side is one attribute because the language has compile-time macros. The Gleam side lacks this mechanism, so the equivalent economy must come from an EXTERNAL codegen or a runtime combinator library, not a derive.
systems.visual_editor: None. Pure compile-time.
systems.agent_support: None native; serde-derived structs are the de-facto way to (de)serialize LLM tool arguments in Rust agent stacks.
top_pain_points: Proc macros CANNOT read type info from other crates — derive sees only the local AST, limiting cross-crate/generic reasoning (serde internals)
Derive macros add real compile-time cost; large serde-heavy crates compile slowly (Rust Internals discussion on improving derive compile times)
No emitted schema/contract artifact — wire compatibility is manual discipline, no Buf-style breaking-change gate
Macro import/hygiene edge cases (serde issue #1441, 2018-edition macro imports)
Attribute config is powerful but sprawling; complex wire mappings become attribute soup
notable_innovations: Derive: type -> codec at compile time with ZERO runtime reflection and hand-written-equal performance — the definitive 'declare once, never write a codec' proof (the bar Aion wants to hit for Gleam)
ONE derive -> every serialization format (format decoupled from the type) via the Serializer/Deserializer trait abstraction
Inline field attributes fold wire-mapping concerns into the type declaration itself

systems.name: Gleam JSON today (the Aion-relevant landscape: no derive, several partial answers)
