systems.authoring_model: Gleam has NO derive/macros, so today the baseline is HAND-WRITTEN pairs: an encoder building `json.object([#("name", json.string(p.name)), ...])` and a SEPARATE decoder using the `gleam/dynamic/decode` combinator API (`use name <- decode.field("name", decode.string) ... decode.success(Person(name:, age:))`). Encoder and decoder are two independent functions that can silently drift. This is exactly Aion's ~20-lines-per-type, shape-described-three-times pain. Several ecosystem approaches try to collapse it (below).
systems.serialization_ergonomics: Four escape routes exist, none yet a clean serde-equivalent: (1) `gleam-community/codec` — ONE bidirectional codec VALUE via a builder (`codec.custom2(...) |> codec.variant3("RGB", RGB, codec.int(), codec.int(), codec.int()) |> codec.construct`) giving encoder+decoder from one declaration (the Effect-Schema model, pure Gleam, runs on BEAM+JS). (2) `convert_json` (Billuc) — a `use`-based converter: one `c.object`/`c.field` definition serves both encode and decode. (3) `gserde` (cdaringe) — build-step CODEGEN: `gleam run -m gserde` parses your types and EMITS `foo_json.gleam` with to_json/from_string (the Encore.ts 'parse types, emit codecs' model), but alpha, incomplete type coverage. (4) `glerd-json` — codegen driven by Glerd (Gleam reflection). PLUS the Gleam COMPILER itself now ships 'Generate JSON encoder' and 'Generate dynamic decoder' code actions (one-shot insertion into source).
systems.determinism_and_durability: Combinator-value approaches (codec/convert_json) give a single reusable definition => no encoder/decoder drift, and the codec is an ordinary value you can version. Codegen approaches (gserde/glerd) emit source you commit, so the wire mapping is inspectable/reviewable — good for durable-history auditing, but regeneration must be re-run on type change (drift risk if forgotten). None has protobuf/Buf-style breaking-change enforcement yet; durable-history safety is still author discipline.
systems.boilerplate_assessment: Combinator libs cut three declarations to ONE (~half to a third of hand-written), but you still author a builder chain per type proportional to fields. Codegen (gserde-style) can reach TRUE zero author codecs — the shape is declared ONCE as the Gleam type, everything else generated — which is the closest analogue to serde/Encore.ts and the strongest fit for Aion's goal. The compiler code actions help but are one-shot inserts that then become hand-maintained (and today's encoder action notably does NOT fail-compile when fields change — a known safety gap, issue #4353).
systems.visual_editor: No visual editor in the Gleam JSON ecosystem. The compiler code actions surface in LSP-enabled editors (VS Code) as quick-fixes, which is the closest thing to tooling-assisted authoring.
systems.agent_support: None specific to JSON tooling; relevant only insofar as reduced codec tax speeds authoring Aion agent-workflow payloads.
top_pain_points: No derive/macros => encoder and decoder are separate hand-written functions that silently drift (the root Aion pain)
Compiler 'Generate JSON encoder' action does NOT re-break on field changes (silently omits new fields) — devs asking for destructuring-based codegen to force compile errors (gleam-lang/gleam #4353)
Same shape described three times (Gleam type + JSON Schema + Rust serde struct) — the exact stacked-dev 2400-line hand codec + unused 3900-line generated file situation
Codegen tools (gserde/glerd-json) are alpha, incomplete type coverage, must be re-run on every type change (regeneration drift)
Combinator libs still cost a builder chain per type and add runtime construction/allocation overhead vs hand-tuned
No Buf-style schema/breaking-change guard for durable workflow-history compatibility
notable_innovations: `gleam-community/codec` + `convert_json`: prove a SINGLE bidirectional codec VALUE (Effect-Schema-style) is achievable in a no-macro language TODAY — one declaration => encoder + decoder, no drift

`gserde` / `glerd-json`: prove the Encore.ts 'parse the language's own types, EMIT codecs' pipeline works for Gleam — path to genuinely ZERO author-written codecs (declare shape once = the Gleam type)
Gleam compiler ships codec code actions natively (LSP quick-fix) — the compiler team already treats codec-generation as a first-class concern Aion can build on / push upstream

Because a Gleam custom type's fields are fully known to the compiler, a build-time codegen (Aion-owned, tied to WorkflowDefinition's input/output/error/signal/query types) could derive ALL boundary codecs AND emit a matching schema for the Rust worker + a durable-history contract — unifying the three descriptions into one

universal_pain_points: VERSIONING / SCHEMA-DRIFT OF IN-FLIGHT WORK is the #1 recurring wound across every replay/journal engine. Temporal (GetVersion branch-bloat + NonDeterministicException surfacing weeks later), Cadence (same GetVersion), Restate (pin-to-immutable-URL, run old+new side-by-side, drain), DBOS ('every workflow finishes on the version it started', retroaction tooling missing), Azure Durable ('Non-Deterministic workflow detected' is the dominant GitHub complaint, sometimes from the HOST not user code), LangGraph ('orphaned thread crisis' — evolving State type crashes suspended threads), Step Functions (25k-event quota, no live migration), Camunda (manual process-instance migration). NOBODY has solved evolving code/types against durable in-flight history well. Only Trigger.dev's atomic versioning (pin run to deploy, don't disturb in-flight) and Protobuf/Buf's field-tag stability + machine-checked breaking-change gate point at real answers. This is the single biggest opportunity for Aion.

THE DETERMINISM FOOTGUN — replay-based engines force the author to police what code is legal in the workflow body (no time/random/UUID/IO/threads), and violations surface LATE, at replay, not at authoring. Temporal, Cadence, Azure Durable (plus JS-must-not-be-async, Py-must-be-generators), and to a lesser degree Restate/DBOS/Inngest ('code outside step.run re-runs every invocation' — Inngest's #1 real-world gotcha: surprise duplicate logs/writes). The two engines that ELIMINATE this — Golem (all non-det captured at the WASI boundary, ordinary imperative code is durable-safe) and Trigger.dev (CRIU checkpoint/restore, code between waits never re-runs) — are the ones authors describe as friendliest. Determinism-transparency is a genuine axis of competition, not a solved constant.

SERIALIZATION BOILERPLATE is concentrated EXACTLY where the host language lacks reflection/derive. Every mainstream engine (Temporal/Restate/DBOS/Inngest/Trigger/Prefect/Dagster/Airflow/Golem/serde) gets codecs for ~free because Go/TS/Python/Rust have reflection or macros. Gleam is the outlier — no derive — which is why Aion's pain (2400 hand codecs + 3900 unused generated + shape described 3×) is unusually acute. This is not a universal problem; it is a Gleam-specific tax the whole industry has already routed around. The lesson: the winning systems make TYPES the source of truth and derive everything else.

VISUAL WORKFLOW ARTIFACTS DESTROY GIT. The moment a workflow becomes a JSON/XML/graph blob, diff/blame/review/merge break. Camunda's .bpmn interleaves logic + layout DI coordinates → a one-attribute change = 257-line diff, merge conflicts can't auto-resolve, spawning custom diff-filters. n8n exported JSON is dominated by node positions/UUIDs/timestamps ('480 of 500 diff lines are position shifts'), no built-in versioning historically, unmanaged merge conflicts. Node-RED flows.json stores Function-node JS as escaped one-line strings + x/y coords, no change attribution. Windmill is the ONLY one that fought this and won — by making the flow plain files (flow.yaml + per-step code files + separated .lock) specifically to make PRs reviewable. Any Aion visual editor MUST keep the textual/file source of truth reviewable.

THE WORKFLOW ↔ WORKER PROCESS SPLIT (orchestrator vs side-effect code in separate processes/registration) is real ceremony that the newest systems collapse. Temporal/Cadence/Azure Durable/Camunda/Step Functions/AION all impose it (4 moving parts: wf + activity + registration + starter). DBOS (in-process library), Inngest (mounts in your existing HTTP app), Restate (handler co-locates durable + effectful code with ctx.run inline), Golem (state mutation is just plain code) all ERASE it. The split is increasingly seen as legacy friction, not essential.

LARGE PAYLOADS / HISTORY BLOAT force manual workarounds everywhere. Step Functions 256KB/state → manual S3 claim-check; 25k-event history cap. Azure Durable large I/O bloats history, risks replay memory exceptions. Airflow XCom is a poor large-data channel (custom backend needed). Golem offloads large payloads to blob with inline refs. Trigger.dev is the gold standard: >512KB auto-uploaded to S3 transparently, author never thinks about it. Aion (content-addressed haematite store) is uniquely positioned to make this invisible.

LOCAL DEV / TESTING FRICTION is chronic. Inngest Dev Server has repeated GitHub issues (HTTPS unsupported, Docker sync failures, ETIMEDOUT, npx cache corruption). Step Functions historically had no real local full-machine run (TestState tests one state at a time). Camunda 8 needs Zeebe+Operate+Tasklist+Optimize running. Temporal self-host needs DB+Elasticsearch+4 services. The engines praised for DX (Temporal's in-memory TestWorkflowEnvironment with time-skipping + OnActivity mocking; Windmill's `wmill dev` live-reload; Mastra's Playground) treat local inner-loop as first-class.

IDEMPOTENCY / SAGA COMPENSATION for external side-effects remains the author's burden even in 'durable' systems — durability guarantees exactly-once REPLAY, not exactly-once EXTERNAL EFFECT. Called out explicitly for Restate, DBOS, and Camunda (at-least-once job delivery → workers must be idempotent). Durability doesn't remove the need to make steps safe to retry.

UNTYPED DATA BETWEEN STEPS in the declarative/visual systems — no compile-time contract that a downstream state/node/step gets the shape an upstream one produces. Step Functions (untyped JSON, JSONPath/JSONata string refs), Camunda (FEEL string var refs, no cross-check), n8n (array-of-items, stringly-typed), Node-RED (msg.payload by convention). Errors surface at runtime, not author time. This is precisely what a typed engine (Gleam) can beat if it carries types THROUGH the boundary.

OPERATIONAL HEAVINESS gates adoption. Temporal/Cadence (DB + Elasticsearch + services, HA-provisioned), DBOS (Postgres bottleneck ~10k steps/sec then shard), Camunda 8 (K8s Zeebe cluster). The pull toward 'library, not infra' (DBOS) and 'mounts in your app' (Inngest) is a clear DX direction.
synthesis.per_axis_best_in_class:
"ECONOMIC TEXTUAL AUTHORING — **DBOS** wins outright (two decorators + ordinary function calls; no worker process, no broker, no codec, no Dynamic-unwrap entrypoint; nearly every line is intent). **Golem** ties it conceptually (6-10 lines of all-intent agent code) but pays a heavy WASM/WIT substrate tax. **Restate** and **Trigger.dev** are close behind. WHY they win: they collapsed the workflow/worker split AND lean on host-language reflection so nothing is glue. This is the exact target shape for Aion: workflow ≈ your types + your order of operations.

SERIALIZATION ERGONOMICS — **Rust serde** is the absolute gold standard (one `#[derive]`, zero runtime reflection, hand-written-equal speed, one derive → all formats) — but it REQUIRES compile-time macros Gleam lacks. For a no-macro language the best-in-class is **Encore.ts** (build-time static analysis of language types → EMITTED schema → precompiled native codec: gets serde-level economy WITHOUT derive macros, AND produces a durable versionable artifact). **Effect Schema** is best-in-class for the runtime-value approach (one declaration = type+validator+encoder+decoder with an explicit `Schema<Type, Encoded, Requirements>` two-way transform — achievable in Gleam today as a composable value).

DETERMINISM TRANSPARENCY — **Golem** wins (ALL non-determinism captured at the WASI boundary and replayed, so ordinary imperative code that calls the clock/random/network/LLM is durable-safe with NO special APIs — the inverse of Azure Durable's forbidden-call list). **Trigger.dev** is a strong second via CRIU process checkpoint/restore (no replay at all, code between waits simply resumes). These prove determinism-transparency is a competitive axis, not a fixed cost.

VISUAL EDITING — **AWS Step Functions Workflow Studio** wins on pure round-trip (genuine live bidirectional Design↔Code: edit the ASL JSON and the canvas updates, drag on canvas and JSON updates, now embeddable in VS Code). **Camunda** wins on visual maturity/vocabulary (BPMN is a decades-refined, portable, business-readable notation) but is crippled by git-diff hell.

CODE↔VISUAL ROUND-TRIP — **Windmill** wins decisively and is the single most relevant prior art for Aion: the flow is plain files (flow.yaml + per-step real code + separated lockfiles) engineered specifically so git diff/PR/blame work, `wmill dev` gives disk↔canvas live-reload, and NEITHER the code nor the canvas is a lossy projection of the other. This is the only system that made a visual workflow properly version-controllable.

AGENT OBSERVABILITY / INTERVENTION — **LangGraph Studio** is best-in-class today (canvas render of live node execution + streaming + TIME-TRAVEL through checkpoints + interrupts/breakpoints + direct STATE EDITING at an interrupt that persists into thread history). **Inngest AgentKit's useAgent** is the best 'durable backend → live frontend' streaming (parts-based, auto-resume-where-it-stopped, zero WebSocket plumbing). Both are the bar Aion's NOI must beat — and both have a clear weakness Aion can exploit (Studio is dev/local + LangChain-only; useAgent's approval-back-to-backend is undocumented).

LOCAL DX — **Temporal** (in-memory TestWorkflowEnvironment with time-skipping + OnActivity mocking — full workflow tests, no live server) and **Windmill** (`wmill dev` bidirectional live-reload) and **Mastra Playground** (chat with agents, hot model-swap, live temp/top-p tuning, per-step traces) share the crown for fast inner loops."
synthesis.serialization_verdict:
"THE VERDICT: For a Gleam engine with no derive macros, the best achievable authoring model is TYPES-AS-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH with a BUILD-TIME CODEGEN step owned by Aion — the Encore.ts pattern ('parse the language's own types, EMIT codecs + a schema'), NOT hand-written combinators and NOT a schema-first IDL. Rust serde proves types-first with zero author codec is the target; Encore.ts proves you can hit serde-level economy WITHOUT language macros by using an external static analyzer + emitted artifact; gserde/glerd-json prove this specific pipeline already works for Gleam. This is the only option that reaches TRUE zero author-written codecs (the shape is declared exactly ONCE — as the Gleam type) while ALSO emitting a durable, versionable contract.

WHY NOT the alternatives:

(1) COMBINATOR VALUES (gleam-community/codec, convert_json — the Effect Schema model in Gleam today). Real and shippable NOW, and they collapse three declarations (type + encoder + decoder) to one bidirectional VALUE with no drift, which is a huge improvement. BUT they still cost a builder chain per type proportional to field count — it is reduced boilerplate, not eliminated boilerplate. Ship this as the INTERIM/manual-override path, but it is not the destination.

(2) SCHEMA-FIRST IDL (protobuf/Smithy). Gives cross-language contracts for free (attractive because Aion is polyglot: Gleam workflow + Rust worker) and Buf's field-tag stability + machine-checked breaking-change gate is the single best answer to durable-history evolution anyone has. BUT it forces the author OUT of Gleam's type system into a separate DSL, describing the shape twice, and there is NO first-class Gleam/BEAM protoc target (you'd write a custom plugin). This inverts Aion's stated goal ('workflow ≈ just your types'). Reject as the primary authoring surface — but STEAL the field-tag/breaking-change discipline for the emitted-artifact layer.

(3) STRUCTURAL INFERENCE (tRPC). Zero declaration overhead, but the inference EVAPORATES at runtime — there is no persistable schema artifact to decode old durable history against. Fatal for a durable engine. Reject.

THE CONCRETE RECOMMENDATION — a build-time tool tied to WorkflowDefinition. Because a Gleam custom type's fields are fully known to the compiler, an Aion-owned codegen keyed on the WorkflowDefinition's input/output/error/signal/query types can, from ONE source (the Gleam types), derive: (a) all boundary codecs on the Gleam side (killing the 2400-line hand-codec tax), (b) a matching schema/serde-compatible contract for the Rust worker (killing the third description and the 3900-line drift), and (c) a durable-history contract with protobuf-style stable-field-id + breaking-change checking so old history decodes against evolved types. This UNIFIES the three descriptions (Gleam type + JSON Schema + Rust struct) into one and directly attacks BOTH the universal versioning wound AND Aion's specific codec tax. Layer the combinator-value library underneath as the escape hatch for exotic/custom wire mappings the codegen can't infer. Push the compiler code-actions upstream but do not rely on them — they are one-shot inserts that famously do NOT re-break on field change (#4353), the exact drift trap to avoid. Regeneration-drift (codegen forgotten after a type change) is the one risk of this path — mitigate by wiring codegen into the build so stale codecs fail compilation, the way Encore's analyzer runs at build time."
synthesis.visual_editor_lessons:
"WHAT MAKES VISUAL EDITING PLEASANT (from the winners):

1. LIVE BIDIRECTIONAL ROUND-TRIP is table stakes — Step Functions Workflow Studio: edit the text, canvas updates; drag the canvas, text updates. A one-way 'diagram-is-source, JSON-is-export' model (n8n, Node-RED) makes the textual artifact useless. Aim for BOTH directions being first-class.

2. FILES-AS-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH is THE lesson, from Windmill (the most directly relevant prior art since Aion is already code). Windmill deliberately made a flow a folder of PLAIN FILES (flow.yaml + per-step real code + SEPARATED lockfiles) specifically so git diff/PR/blame/merge work. They even split lockfiles out of the flow YAML because inline lockfiles made diffs noisy. Aion's advantage: workflows are ALREADY Gleam code — do NOT regress into a JSON/graph blob. The visual editor must be a VIEW over reviewable text, never the source.

3. NEVER INTERLEAVE LOGIC WITH LAYOUT. Camunda's .bpmn XML embeds Diagram Interchange coordinates alongside logic, so nudging a box = 257-line diff and unresolvable merge conflicts. If Aion stores layout, keep it in a SEPARATE file/sidecar (or derive layout deterministically from the code so there's nothing to diff). This single decision determines whether the visual editor is git-usable.

4. SEMANTIC DIFF over textual diff. Camunda Web Modeler added visual version-diff + changelists because git-diffing a declarative artifact is the wrong altitude. Aion should offer a semantic/structural diff of workflow shape, not raw text churn.

5. LIVE PER-EDGE DATA + 'PIN DATA'. n8n's best-loved features: per-node input/output data panels (you always see the actual shape on each wire — makes an untyped system tolerable) and 'pin data' (freeze a node's real output so you iterate downstream without re-hitting APIs). Aion should mirror pin-data for activities (freeze a real activity result while iterating the workflow) — a killer inner-loop ergonomic, and Aion's durable history already HAS the real recorded values.

6. WHERE VISUAL BREAKS AND MUST DROP TO CODE: the universal failure is that real logic HIDES OFF-CANVAS. Camunda's conditions/mappings live in FEEL fields inside property panels, so 'the diagram tells the whole story' erodes as complexity grows; n8n/Node-RED dilute ~15 lines of code into 40-node spaghetti. LESSON: the canvas should own COARSE control flow (sequence, branch, parallel, fan-out, wait/HITL, sub-workflow) — the order-of-operations — and DROP TO CODE (real Gleam) for any non-trivial expression/condition/transform. Don't try to visually encode business logic; encode topology. Windmill's input-transform model (step fields are small inspectable JS expressions over flow_input/results.<id>, with a visual 'plug' picker) is the right altitude for data-wiring.

7. DON'T force visual on people who want code (and vice versa). Windmill lets you author entirely in the canvas OR entirely in files and round-trips. Since Aion authors are Gleam devs, code-first with an OPTIONAL visual view/overlay (and visual authoring for coarse structure) is the right default — not a mandatory canvas."
synthesis.agent_authoring_lessons:
"THE MARKET STRUCTURE (the key strategic insight): the space has cleanly split into (a) DURABILITY SUBSTRATES (Temporal, Restate, DBOS, Golem) that say 'agents are just durable workflows, bring your own agent UX' and (b) AGENT FRAMEWORKS (LangGraph, Mastra, AgentKit) that provide authoring + observability but have WEAK or dev-only durability (LangGraph's checkpoints are explicitly 'not durable execution' per Diagrid — no watchdog/heartbeat, manual resume, no coordination against double-resume of a thread_id). THE UNCLAIMED INTERSECTION is: durable, distributed, guaranteed-completion execution WITH native LIVE agent observability + intervention. That intersection is exactly Aion's NOI + haematite + kill-9 failover. This is the leapfrog lane — nobody owns it.

BEST-IN-CLASS TODAY, AND THEIR EXPLOITABLE GAPS:

• LangGraph Studio is the observability/intervention bar: live node-execution transcript, streaming, TIME-TRAVEL through checkpoints, interrupts/breakpoints, and direct STATE EDITING at an interrupt that PERSISTS into thread history (traceable). GAPS Aion can beat: Studio's replay is LOCAL/dev-centric (not durable/distributed), it's LangChain-ONLY, and its durability is snapshot-not-guaranteed. Aion can offer the SAME time-travel/state-edit/interrupt UX but backed by DURABLE, distributed, crash-survivable history (haematite) and language-agnostic.

• Inngest AgentKit's useAgent is the best 'durable backend → live frontend' streaming: parts-based (text/tool/reasoning/data parts update independently), AUTO-RESUME exactly where execution stopped, zero WebSocket/state-store plumbing for the dev. GAP: how frontend approvals/interruptions flow BACK to the durable backend is UNDOCUMENTED. Aion's NOI already targets explicit inject-message/cancel — make the intervention channel first-class and documented and you beat useAgent on the exact axis it's weakest.

CONCRETE LESSONS TO STEAL:

1. AUTO-DERIVE TOOL SCHEMAS FROM TYPED SIGNATURES. Temporal's activity_as_tool and Golem's typegen auto-generate the LLM tool schema from the activity's typed signature — no hand-written tool schema. This is the SAME codegen Aion needs for its codec tax: the build-time tool that emits codecs from Gleam types should ALSO emit OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible tool schemas for activities-used-as-tools. One derivation, three payoffs (codec + worker contract + agent tool schema).

2. HITL AS A FIRST-CLASS DURABLE PRIMITIVE that costs ~zero while waiting. Restate awakeables, Trigger.dev waitpoint tokens, Azure Durable's event-raced-with-timer, Temporal Signals/Updates, AgentKit waitForEvent (pauses DAYS without holding resources). Aion should make mid-run intervention (inject-message, approve/reject, cancel) a durable awaitable that consumes no compute while pending — and surface it BOTH in the ops-console AND (per the parked CLI-parity idea) as `aion intervene`.

3. AGENTS-AS-STEPS THAT COMPOSE WITH NORMAL CONTROL FLOW. Windmill's AI-agent steps compose with branches/approval/error-handling like any other step and can call workflow functions as tools; Restate/DBOS wrap existing agent SDKs without a proprietary graph ('agents are just code'). Do NOT force a proprietary agent-graph DSL — let a Gleam agent loop be ordinary code where each LLM/tool call is a durable step, observable in NOI.

4. LLM NON-DETERMINISM IS A FEATURE OF THE STEP MODEL, NOT A PROBLEM. Inngest markets that LLM calls are 'naturally a step' with no replay-isolation to design — recording the LLM RESPONSE in durable history (Golem's DurableOpenAI, Restate's journaled step) makes an inherently non-deterministic call replay-safe and exactly-once. Aion's content-addressed haematite store is ideal for recording+deduplicating LLM/tool responses.

THE LEAPFROG THESIS: Aion is the only stack positioned to unify (durable + distributed + crash-survivable execution) with (LangGraph-Studio-grade live transcript + time-travel + state-edit + intervention) with (AgentKit-grade durable frontend streaming) — because NOI + haematite + liminal + kill-9 failover already exist. Make the intervention channel (inject-message/cancel/approve) a documented, durable, bidirectional first-class primitive, back the time-travel with real durable history rather than dev-local checkpoints, and auto-derive tool schemas from Gleam types via the same codegen that kills the codec tax."

proposal.thesis: Best-in-class economic authoring for Aion means an author declares each boundary shape EXACTLY ONCE and writes ONLY order-of-operations plus real business logic — no run-ceremony, no hand-codecs, no third description of the same data. Aion sits in an unclaimed quadrant no incumbent occupies: DBOS/Golem/Restate give near-zero-ceremony textual authoring but NO durable-distributed live-agent observability+intervention; LangGraph Studio/AgentKit give live agent UX but snapshot-not-durable, dev-local, single-language. Aion is the only stack that already has (a) a typed workflow language (Gleam) with a real type system, (b) polyglot durable workers (Rust/Python), (c) a content-addressed durable store (haematite), AND (d) a durable+real-time observability/intervention plane (NOI). The positioning: 'The durable-execution engine where a workflow is just your types and your order of operations, every in-flight run is a live editable transcript backed by real crash-survivable history, and the same ONE type declaration drives the Gleam codec, the Rust/Python worker contract, the agent tool schema, AND the durable-history compatibility gate.' We beat Temporal/Restate/DBOS by matching their textual economy AND owning live durable agent intervention; we extend past LangGraph Studio by making time-travel/state-edit back onto REAL distributed history instead of dev-local checkpoints.
proposal.textual_authoring_redesign:
Two distinct taxes, two distinct fixes, both grounded in files I read.

KILL THE RUN-CEREMONY (pure SDK + package change, no codegen). Today even the 'generated' order-saga hand-writes ~27 lines of `run(raw_input: Dynamic)` that decodes the Dynamic-as-string, runs the input codec, calls `execute`, re-encodes output/error (confirmed in examples/hello-world/src/hello_world.gleam and examples/order-saga/src/order_saga.gleam:56). Yet `workflow.define(...)` ALREADY bundles name+input_codec+output_codec+error_codec+entry_fn into a `WorkflowDefinition` (gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/workflow.gleam) and the package layer ALREADY reads the typed entry as the last arg of `workflow.define(...)` (crates/aion-package/src/structure/facts.rs). The fix: the SDK exposes ONE adapter, `workflow.entrypoint(definition) -> fn(Dynamic) -> Result(String, String)`, that performs the identical decode/run/encode dance the engine needs, driven entirely by the codecs already inside the WorkflowDefinition. The engine's `run/1` binding then becomes a single generated (or one-line hand-written) shim: `pub fn run(raw) { workflow.entrypoint(definition())(raw) }`. This deletes the 27-line branch-per-failure block from every workflow with ZERO change to the engine boundary contract (still `run(raw_input: Dynamic)`), and it's the single highest-leverage, lowest-risk change.

KILL/RELIEVE THE CODEC TAX. The tax is Gleam-specific (no serde derive) and acute: examples/stacked-dev/src/aion_stacked_dev_io.gleam is 3935 lines and the flagship order-fulfillment still hand-writes 400+ lines of paired to_json/decoder in order_types.gleam. Aion has ALREADY chosen the right mechanism — `aion generate` turns `schemas/*.json` into io+codecs+worker+wire-goldens+manifest (docs/authoring.md), and order-saga proves it works. The redesign is to (1) finish migrating authoring onto that generated path so NO project hand-writes codecs, and (2) resolve the source-of-truth tension below so the schema isn't a hand-written THIRD artifact.

TARGET MINIMAL WORKFLOW (hello-world, after redesign) — the whole module:

  import aion/workflow
  import app/types.{type HelloInput, type Greeting}   // types + codecs generated

  pub fn definition() {
    workflow.define("hello", types.hello_input_codec(), types.greeting_codec(), types.err_codec(), execute)
  }
  pub fn run(raw) { workflow.entrypoint(definition())(raw) }   // the only glue line

  fn execute(in: HelloInput) -> Result(Greeting, WorkflowError) {
    use g <- result.try(workflow.run(greet(in)))   // one activity, typed
    Ok(g)
  }

Compare to today's 90-line hello_world.gleam. `execute` is pure intent; `run` is one line; every codec is generated; the codec module is never hand-touched. That is the DBOS/Golem economy, reached in a no-macro language via Aion-owned codegen — exactly the Encore.ts pattern the synthesis endorses.
proposal.serialization_recommendation:
FIRM RECOMMENDATION: types-as-source-of-truth via Aion-owned BUILD-TIME codegen keyed on the Gleam workflow module — but reached through a deliberate, honest migration from the current schema-first mechanism, because Aion has ALREADY shipped schema-first and the codebase carries a stated-vs-actual contradiction that must be resolved before more workflows are written.

The contradiction I found: ADR-014 (docs/guides/aion-authoring-dx.md) declares 'the typed Gleam workflow module is the single source of truth… no DSL and no second schema,' but the actual shipped `aion generate` (docs/authoring.md) makes `schemas/*.json` the source and GENERATES the Gleam types from it — the schema IS a second, hand-authored artifact. So the shape is still described twice (JSON Schema + the Rust/Python worker struct), just not thrice. This is better than the order-fulfillment hand-codec path but is NOT the stated ideal.

Recommendation, Aion-specific: make the GLEAM TYPE the single declared shape and have Aion's codegen (which already parses `workflow.define(...)` via the facts tokeniser in crates/aion-package/src/structure/facts.rs) EMIT: (a) the Gleam boundary codecs (killing the 3935-line tax), (b) a JSON-Schema/serde-compatible contract consumed by the Rust worker (serde derive) and Python worker (killing the third description), and (c) a durable-history contract carrying protobuf-style STABLE FIELD IDs + a `buf breaking`-style compatibility check so old haematite history decodes against evolved types. One source → four artifacts. Because Gleam has no derive macros, this MUST be external codegen wired into `gleam build`/`aion check` so a stale codec fails compilation (the Encore-analyzer-at-build-time discipline; regeneration-drift is the one real risk and this is its mitigation).

Migration path from today's 3x-described state: Step 1 — keep schema-first `aion generate` exactly as-is (it works, order-saga proves it) but retarget the codegen INPUT from `schemas/*.json` to the Gleam type declarations, emitting the same io/codecs/worker/goldens outputs. Authors delete their schemas/ and their type is authoritative; the diff to the generated outputs should be near-empty, which is how you verify the swap. Step 2 — migrate order-fulfillment (the flagship, still on hand-codecs) onto the generated path, deleting order_types.gleam's hand-written codecs. Step 3 — add stable-field-ids + the breaking-change gate to the emitted history contract. Keep the combinator-value codec library (aion/codec) UNDERNEATH as the documented escape hatch for exotic wire mappings the codegen can't infer — never the primary surface.
novel_bets: PIN-DATA FROM REAL DURABLE HISTORY (haematite content-addressing). n8n's most-loved inner-loop feature is 'pin data' — freeze a node's real output so you iterate downstream without re-hitting APIs. Aion can do this NATIVELY and better: every activity result is already a content-addressed value in haematite. 'Replay this workflow but pin activity charge_payment to the exact recorded result from run X' becomes a store lookup by content hash — no re-execution, no mock, the REAL prior value. No incumbent has real-durable pin-data because none has a content-addressed history; LangGraph's time-travel is dev-local checkpoints.

TIME-TRAVEL + STATE-EDIT ON DISTRIBUTED DURABLE HISTORY, not dev checkpoints. LangGraph Studio's time-travel/interrupt/state-edit is the observability bar but is local, LangChain-only, and snapshot-not-durable (Diagrid's 'checkpoints are not durable execution'). Aion's NOI + haematite + kill-9 failover can offer the SAME UX backed by real crash-survivable, cross-node history. The NOI design already locks 'intervention is a durable observability record but NOT part of replay' (NORN-OBSERVABILITY-AND-INTERVENTION.md) — that exact separation is what makes state-edit safe against replay, which LangGraph gets wrong (its orphaned-thread schema-drift crisis).

ONE TYPE DECLARATION → AGENT TOOL SCHEMA, FOR FREE. Temporal's activity_as_tool and Golem's typegen auto-derive the LLM tool JSON-schema from a typed activity signature. Aion's boundary codegen already must parse activity input/output types; make it ALSO emit OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible tool schemas for activities used as agent tools. One derivation, four payoffs: Gleam codec + Rust/Python worker contract + durable-history gate + agent tool schema. This directly fuses the codec-tax fix with the agent story via the NOI JSON-RPC intervene/approval channel already designed.

TYPES CARRIED THROUGH THE BOUNDARY beat every untyped visual engine. Step Functions/Camunda/n8n/Node-RED all lose the type contract between steps (runtime failures, not author-time). Because Aion workflows are Gleam and the boundary codecs are generated from the same types the Rust worker validates via serde, Aion can guarantee a compile-time-checked contract end-to-end — a genuine differentiator the moment a visual layer exists: the canvas shows real typed wires, not stringly-typed JSONPath.

DURABLE-HISTORY-SAFE EVOLUTION via content-addressed module namespacing + stable field ids. The #1 universal wound (versioning in-flight work) is unsolved by everyone except Trigger.dev's atomic pin and Buf's field-tag gate. Aion already has content-hash module namespacing (logical_name$hash pins in-flight runs to their version — aion-authoring-dx.md invariant 5). Combine that with protobuf-style stable field ids on the emitted history contract and a machine-checked breaking-change gate, and Aion can claim the strongest evolution story in the category — old haematite history decodes against evolved types by construction, checked in CI.
proposal.visual_dx_roadmap:
Staged, each phase shipping standalone value; reuse the existing React ops-console and NOI streams rather than building a new canvas from scratch.

PHASE 0 (already partly here) — LIVE RUN VISUALISER over NOI. The ops-console + NOI transcript streaming already exist (React ops-console, NOI JSON-RPC event/* notifications). First visual artifact is READ-ONLY: render a running workflow as a live graph — activities, timers, signals, child workflows, agent steps — driven by the NOI event stream and the recorded history. This is DBOS-Conductor/Step-Functions-graph parity and is the lowest-risk, highest-demo-value first cut. Learn from LangGraph Studio (inspection-first) and DBOS Conductor (exec-graph + lifecycle ops).

PHASE 1 — INTERVENTION + TIME-TRAVEL on the live visualiser. Wire the NOI intervene/*, approval/*, and cancel channels (already designed and locked) into the graph: pause at a boundary, inject a steer, approve/reject, cancel. Add time-travel scrubbing over the durable history and content-addressed pin-data (novel bet #1). This is where Aion PASSES LangGraph Studio — same UX, durable+distributed backing. HITL as a durable awaitable that costs zero while pending (steal Restate awakeables / Trigger waitpoints).

PHASE 2 — SEMANTIC (NOT TEXTUAL) DIFF + DEPLOY VIEW. Because workflows are Gleam code (files-as-source-of-truth already, the Windmill lesson won for free), offer a structural diff of workflow SHAPE (topology/boundary-types) rather than raw text churn — and surface the content-hash version pinning so operators see which in-flight runs are on which version. Camunda added semantic diff because git-diffing a declarative blob is the wrong altitude; Aion avoids the blob entirely but still wants shape-diff for review.

PHASE 3 — BIDIRECTIONAL CODE<->VISUAL for COARSE TOPOLOGY ONLY. The hard, later bet. Learn the universal lesson (visual_editor_lessons): the canvas owns COARSE control flow (sequence/branch/parallel/fan-out/wait-HITL/child-workflow — the order-of-operations) and DROPS TO REAL GLEAM for any non-trivial expression/condition/transform. NEVER interleave layout with logic: derive layout deterministically from the code (nothing to diff) or keep it in a sidecar. Round-trip like Step Functions Studio (edit either, both update) and Windmill (`wmill dev` disk<->canvas), but keep Gleam the source of truth — the visual is a VIEW, never a lossy projection. This is optional-not-mandatory because Aion authors are Gleam devs; code-first with a visual overlay is the right default.
proposal.sequencing:
Relieve textual authoring FIRST so the imminent NOI-proof workflow is written on the clean path, without boiling the ocean.

CUT 1 (small, days, do before the NOI-proof workflow) — KILL THE RUN-CEREMONY. Add `workflow.entrypoint(definition)` to the SDK (pure Gleam, drives the codecs already in WorkflowDefinition) and make the engine `run/1` shim one line. This is self-contained, needs no engine change (contract stays `run(raw_input: Dynamic)`), and immediately deletes the 27-line block from every workflow including the NOI-proof one. Write the NOI-proof workflow with `execute` + a one-line `run` from day one.

CUT 2 (small-medium, in parallel) — ENSURE THE NOI-PROOF WORKFLOW USES GENERATED CODECS. Do NOT hand-write codecs for it. Use the existing schema-first `aion generate` path (proven by order-saga) so it ships with zero hand-codecs even before the source-of-truth swap lands. This gets the imminent proof onto the clean path today.

CUT 3 (medium, the real source-of-truth initiative, AFTER the proof ships) — retarget codegen from `schemas/*.json` to the Gleam type declarations (novel serialization recommendation), verify by near-empty diff of generated outputs, then migrate order-fulfillment off hand-codecs. This is the bigger 'declare once' work and must not block the NOI proof.

CUT 4 (larger, parallel track once CUT 3 stabilises) — VISUAL Phase 0/1: the live NOI run-visualiser + intervention/time-travel, reusing the ops-console. This is the differentiator demo and depends only on NOI+history, not on the codegen work.

CUT 5 (later) — stable-field-ids + breaking-change gate on the history contract, then bidirectional code<->visual (Phase 2/3). These are the ambitious long-tail; sequence them last.

Rule of thumb: CUTs 1+2 unblock the imminent proof and are cheap; CUT 3 is the strategic textual win; CUTs 4-5 are the visual/versioning moat. Never let 3/4/5 delay shipping the NOI proof.
risks_and_open_questions: GLEAM CODEGEN FEASIBILITY: the facts extractor (crates/aion-package/src/structure/facts.rs) is a TOKENISER, deliberately 'not a Gleam type-checker.' Deriving full boundary codecs from Gleam types needs real type resolution (nested custom types, generics, options, unions/variants). This is materially harder than reading the last arg of workflow.define. Open question: build a proper Gleam type reader, lean on the Gleam compiler/LSP, or keep schema-first as the pragmatic 'declare once' compromise. Honest risk: the pure types-as-truth ideal may be a multi-quarter build; schema-first-but-single may be the shippable 90%.

REGENERATION-DRIFT is the core risk of any codegen path (synthesis flags it explicitly). Mitigation is wiring codegen into build/`aion check --check` so stale codecs fail compilation — Aion already does this for the schema path (`aion generate --check` in CI), so the discipline exists, but it must extend to the types-as-truth swap.

DETERMINISM MUST BE PRESERVED. Every change (entrypoint adapter, codegen, visual pin-data/state-edit) must not violate invariant 2 (workflow is a pure function of recorded history) or invariant 3 (single writer per workflow). The entrypoint adapter is pure decode/encode so it's safe; but time-travel STATE-EDIT and pin-data touch history semantics — the NOI design's 'intervention is durable-but-not-replay' rule is the guardrail and must be honored exactly, or you recreate LangGraph's orphaned-thread bug.

SCOPE CREEP vs SHIPPING THE NOI PROOF. The visual editor, bidirectional round-trip, and stable-field-id evolution gate are each large. The stated immediate need is the NOI-proof workflow Tom drives through the UI. Risk: the codegen/visual initiatives delay it. Sequencing (CUTs 1+2 only) is designed to prevent this — resist pulling CUT 3+ forward.

SOURCE-OF-TRUTH POLITICS: ADR-014 says types-first but the shipped code is schema-first. Someone must OWN the decision to retarget codegen (or to amend ADR-014 to bless schema-first-as-single-source). Leaving the contradiction unresolved means new workflows keep landing on inconsistent paths (order-fulfillment hand-codecs vs order-saga generated).

VISUAL LAYOUT/DIFF: if any visual layer ever persists coordinates, it must keep them out of the Gleam source (sidecar or derived) or Aion re-imports Camunda's git-diff-hell. Decide the layout-derivation strategy before building the canvas, not after.

POLYGLOT CONTRACT SKEW: emitting one contract for Gleam codecs AND Rust serde AND Python workers AND agent tool schemas is powerful but multiplies the surface that must stay byte-compatible with recorded history. The wire-compat goldens (already generated per value type) are the guard; ensure they extend to every emitted target.
